The Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP) is the most inclusive
intergovernmental platform in the Asia-Pacific region. The Commission promotes cooperation
among its 53 member States and 9 associate members in pursuit of solutions to sustainable
development challenges. ESCAP is one of the five regional commissions of the United Nations.
The ESCAP secretariat supports inclusive, resilient and sustainable development in the region
by generating action-oriented knowledge, and by providing technical assistance and
capacity-building services in support of national development objectives, regional agreements
and the implementation of the 2030 Agenda for Sustainable Development.

chunks.text:
Foreword
Extreme heat is a familiar challenge for many people in Asia and
the Pacific. Vulnerable communities throughout the region have
experienced its devastating effects, whether in urban areas where
health, daily life and productivity are affected, or in rural regions
where prolonged heatwaves and droughts can destroy crops and
livelihoods. Only in recent years have governments begun to focus on
the dangers posed by extreme heat, as they increasingly acknowledge
the relentless impact of climate change.
Despite the long-term and transboundary risks associated with these
heat events, national and international responses have typically been
reactive and short-term, focusing on immediate relief or support for
those most affected. Moreover, the measurement of extreme heat
has often been inconsistent, lacking globally accepted metrics for
identifying severity thresholds.
The 2025 edition of the Asia-Pacific Disaster Report, “Rising Heat,
Rising Risk,” calls on governments to develop clear long-term action
plans to address this phenomenon. These plans should be informed
by advances in science, operationalized through technological
innovations and guided by urgent human resilience priorities, while
fostering regional cooperation. Heatwaves should be central to all
multi-hazard planning, with well-prepared early warning systems that
utilize smart technologies and ensure that no one is left behind. In
this context, AI applications are increasingly broadening our policy
frameworks for regional resilience.
The sun’s rays strike the Earth without regard for national borders.
As we confront rising temperatures, rising risks underline the need
to strengthen regional cooperation in sharing data, innovations
and best practices that can bolster resilience for all. The report
proposes a number of solutions, among which are three initiatives
for implementation by ESCAP at the regional level. I hope the
analysis, findings and recommendations inspire policymakers to
adopt transformational, system-wide strategies that are informed
by a shared understanding of the current heat crisis and the report’s
troubling long-term projections.
Armida Salsiah Alisjahbana
Under-Secretary-General of the United Nations
and Executive Secretary of ESCAP
iii

chunks.text:
Executive summary
1 The events and numbers are taken from EM-DAT and pertain to the period up to the end of 2024.
2 While there are differing scenarios with opinion divided on their probabilities — given that these are ultimately projections — the Report’s use of
the SSP5-8.5 scenario is based on several key considerations. SSP5-8.5 has long been employed as a “business-as-usual” scenario within the scientific
community. Moreover, worst-case risk planning is standard practice in disaster risk reduction (DRR), where even low-probability but high-impact
scenarios must be taken into account. Importantly, even assuming full implementation of current national policies and commitments under the NDCs
– an increasingly uncertain assumption – the world remains on track for approximately 2.5°C to 2.9°C of warming by 2100, as highlighted by the 2023
Emissions Gap Report from the UN Environment Programme (UNEP).
The sun’s rays strike the Earth without regard for national borders. In Asia and the Pacific, rising temperatures
are impacting all, everywhere. Disaster risks are expanding and intensifying as temperatures rise with serious
consequences not only for human health but also for socio-economic-environmental systems. The frequency
and scale of weather events resulting from rising temperatures can range from extensive floods to intense
storms, to prolonged drought, expanding desertification and giving rise to new corridors of sand and dust
storms. In fact, the fastest growing climate hazards are from rising temperatures.
Rising Heat, Rising Risk assesses recent as well as long-term trends. Based on the latest projections, it distills
cascading impacts for multiple infrastructures, and interrelated food, water and energy systems. It identifies
troubling impacts on health systems, labour productivity, livelihoods and human well-being, with vulnerable
people among those disproportionately impacted by these projections. Furthermore, the report identifies
important opportunities for solutions and policy actions. In this regard, countries will have to plan strategically
for heat resilience at both local and national levels, while also taking full advantage of opportunities for
regional cooperation. The report underlines that as climate change, rising temperatures and risk is a global
phenomenon, policy coordination at the regional level will be key to advancing multilateral cooperation for
human resilience to disasters. Towards this end, the report finds that dedicated funding may not be needed.
Instead financing for heat resilience requires strategic integration into existing adaptation plans, development
funding and policy streams, and guided by advances in science, technology, innovation and data-sharing at
national, regional and global levels. 1
High temperatures are driving an expanding and intensifying disaster riskscape
Asia and the Pacific was impacted by various natural hazards and climate-induced extreme events in 2024 and 2025,
and the fastest growing climate-related hazards are driven by extreme heat. The year 2024 was the hottest on record
globally, with most countries in Asia and the Pacific having experienced episodes of extreme heat. The disaster which
affected the greatest number of people was a heatwave in Bangladesh in April and May 2024, with around 33 million
people affected. The heatwave in India was the second deadliest event, with around 700 fatalities.
To assess future heat risks, the Report uses the ‘heat index’, which registers how hot it feels when relative humidity
is also taken into account. A heat index of 35°C and above counts as severe heat stress and prolonged exposure
at this level poses health dangers. At 41°C, prolonged exposure represents extreme danger when a heat stroke
becomes likely.
Under the IPCC’s high-emissions climate change scenario (SSP5–8.5),2 extreme and severe heat conditions will
increase dramatically in frequency, intensity and geographic extent by the end of the century. The number of days
exceeding the critical thresholds of 35°C or 41°C will rise substantially in nearly all subregions, transforming what were
once episodic events into chronic seasonal or even year-round hazards.
Disproportionate impacts for the most vulnerable people, economies and ecosystems
Rising temperatures and related hazards are already disrupting people’s lives, adversely affecting economies
and degrading ecosystems. Extreme heat triggers cascading impacts: it directly threatens health and can reduce
economic productivity, with attendant negative impacts on food-energy-water systems, potentially damaging critical
infrastructure and accelerating environmental degradation while deepening existing inequalities.
Urban areas are particularly high-risk locations due to the urban heat island effect. Densely built surfaces trap heat
and elevate city temperatures above those of surrounding rural areas. Many Asian cities such as Seoul, Tokyo, Beijing,
Delhi, Karachi, Dhaka, Manila, Jakarta and Phnom Penh are projected to be substantially hotter in the years ahead, with
this effect adding an extra 2°C-7°C on top of global warming. As access to cooling, water and healthcare come under
increased strain, children, the elderly and outdoor low-wage earners in poor and densely populated urban areas are
disproportionately affected. Higher-income areas usually lie in cooler, greener neighbourhoods, exacerbating issues
of equity and environmental justice. For example, a study in Bandung, Indonesia, found a temperature difference of
up to 7°C between the hottest and coolest neighbourhoods.

chunks.text:
Hazards to health – Extreme heat disrupts the body’s thermoregulation, straining cardiovascular and respiratory systems
and exacerbating underlying health conditions. Heat-related illnesses include cardiovascular and respiratory disease,
kidney injury and heatstroke. Heat-induced trauma has also been linked to long-term cognitive and cardiovascular
impacts. Under all future climate scenarios, heat stress is projected to rise sharply across Asia and the Pacific, with
disproportionate health impacts on the most vulnerable groups, such as children, the elderly and the poor. At the
same time, health facilities may not be able to provide services and protect people if they are located in multi-hazard
zones, particularly in coastal and low-lying regions as is the case for many countries in South and South-East Asia.
Economic impacts – Intensifying multi-hazard risks, including earthquakes, tsunamis, landslides, floods and cyclonic
storms threaten much of the regions’ critical infrastructure – transport, energy, water, telecommunications, schools
and hospitals. Under SSP5-8.5, total losses are expected to rise to US$ 498 billion. To these should be added the impacts
of heat stress, notably on labour productivity: across Asia and the Pacific, between 1995 and 2030 working hours lost to
heat stress are projected to more than double, from 3.75 million to over 8.1 million full-time job equivalents.
Vulnerabilities in agriculture – Agriculture is now under unprecedented pressure from rising temperatures – where
it is reducing crop yields, livestock productivity, labour capacity and productivity, while entrenching rural poverty
traps. ESCAP has developed an ‘agricultural heat stress score’, which shows five countries consistently at high risk:
Afghanistan, India, Pakistan, Nepal and Bangladesh. Some Pacific Island countries such as Fiji, Papua New Guinea and
the Marshall Islands, demonstrate moderate to high future exposure.
Vulnerabilities for energy – Energy infrastructure is often the first to feel the impact of rising temperatures with surges
in electricity demand for air-conditioning and cooling. Left unchecked, energy consumption from air-conditioners
is projected to more than triple by 2050. But, extreme heat itself weakens energy generation: high air temperatures
reduce the efficiency of power production and transmission. Under future climate scenarios, the proportion of power
plants exposed to days exceeding 40°C climbs markedly and long-term exposure more than doubles to 8 per cent.
A warmer future everywhere with glacial melt as one of the most disastrous consequences
for the planet’s core ecosystems
Global warming, driven by rising greenhouse gases, is impacting the Earth’s oceans, land, glaciers and atmosphere.
Approximately 91 per cent of the heat accumulated in the climate system is absorbed by the oceans, while the
remaining warming is distributed among the land (5 per cent), melting ice (3 per cent) and the atmosphere (1 per cent).
Glaciers – One of the most disastrous consequences of global warming is the melting of glaciers. It exacerbates
changes in tele-connected systems of oceans and atmosphere across distant regions – disrupting monsoon patterns,
snow and melt dynamics, and permafrost thaw which all leads to the increased frequency, intensity and expansion of
floods, landslides, droughts, land degradation and sand and dust storms. Globally, glaciers have lost about 5 per cent
of their volume this century.
Furthermore, out of 58 countries in Asia and the Pacific, 17 contain glaciers which can melt into lakes that burst their
banks leading to sudden glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs). The glaciers with the highest GLOF risks are in High
Mountain Asia, where a total of 9.3 million people are exposed to 2,211 glacial lakes. By 2060, countries like the Islamic
Republic of Iran, Mongolia, Myanmar, Türkiye and Uzbekistan could lose over 70 per cent of their glacier mass under
the SSP5-8.5 scenario, posing GLOF risks.
Oceans – A major consequence of global warming is sea level rise. In 2023, the global average sea level attained its
highest point since satellite observations began in 1993, driven by ongoing ocean warming and the accelerated loss
of ice from glaciers and ice sheets. Under the SSP5-8.5 scenario, American Samoa, Samoa, the Philippines, Japan and
Tonga show the largest absolute changes in sea level between 2005 and 2050, listed in declining order, as will be the
case for the rest of the Executive Summary. The largest increase in sea level is expected in the Pacific, closely followed
by East and North-East Asia.
Land – Higher temperatures evaporate more surface water, drying out soils and vegetation, leading to increasingly
frequent droughts and water shortages, and in the longer term to desertification. The capacity of the land to act as
a carbon sink can also be affected by changes in weather patterns. For the period 2041–2060, the Asia-Pacific countries
projected to be most arid are – Mongolia, Pakistan, Uzbekistan, Islamic Republic of Iran and Turkmenistan.
Atmosphere – Higher temperatures evaporate more water from oceans, lakes and land. Every 1°C rise in air temperature
is associated with a 7 per cent increase in the air’s capacity to hold water vapour. This results in more intense
precipitation, including heavier rainfall and more frequent, larger storms. Under the SSP5-8.5 scenario, Bangladesh,
Samoa, Myanmar, American Samoa and Brunei Darussalam are expected to have the highest precipitation during their
wettest quarters.
The multifaceted challenges that countries face due to global warming can be assessed using a composite index
covering the risks for oceans, land, glaciers and atmosphere. Under the SSP5-8.5 scenario, Myanmar, the Islamic Republic
of Iran, American Samoa, India and Türkiye are expected to face the largest composite risks. Of the subregions, the
largest composite risk is for the Pacific followed by South-East Asia and East and North-East Asia, while the composite
risk is much lower in North and Central Asia.

chunks.text:
Planning for heat resilience: From the short-term, reactive approaches, to long-term, system-wide
strategies
Heat resilience refers to the capacity of a system to anticipate, withstand, adapt to and recover from extreme heat in
ways that maintain its essential functions – protecting people’s health and livelihoods, and strengthening institutional
capacities, local economies, infrastructure and the environment. Heat risk arises when periods of extreme heat coincide
with high exposure and vulnerability. However, because the terms heatwave, heat stress and heat risk are often used
interchangeably, WMO and WHO are promoting a more standardized and harmonized approach. Heatwaves are now
defined as: local cumulative excess heat during a sequence of unusually hot days and nights. Having a more precise
and common understanding, both in-country and across-countries, of the metrics of heatwaves and heat stress is
important for quantifying heat risk and consequently devising the transformational system wide strategies further
discussed below.
A key challenge in building heat resilience is that the current policy approach to heat hazards across the Asia-Pacific
region is mostly short-term and reactive. Authorities issue warnings, assess the toll on communities and economies,
and provide some relief – incremental approaches that are ill-suited for driving systemic change or reforming
underlying structures. Instead, Asia and the Pacific needs an integrated regional heat-resilience framework that turns
the science discussed in chapters 1-4 into coordinated, financed action. The framework takes health protection as the
starting point and it is organized around mutually reinforcing thrusts, underpinned by integrated financing:
1. Heat-ready multi-hazard early warning systems (MHEWS) as shared services: Agreed alert levels tied to common
metrics on heat, interoperable alerting and local-language communication.
2. Health-centric standard steps: Standard clinical steps, including continuity during patient surges, for hospitals and
clinics to follow during heat alerts.
3. Integrated planning for cascading risks, with oceans and glaciers treated as systems impacted by heat: Cryosphere-
aware seasonal outlooks that drive anticipatory water-energy-food operations related to reservoirs, irrigation
calendars, planting advisories and similar measures.
4. Equity-first social protection: Climate-responsive social protection with income and non-income support triggered
by heat indices. This includes remunerated provisions for rest-shade-water, flexible working hours and targeted
protocols for women, older persons, children, persons with disabilities, migrants and outdoor workers.
5. Integrated financing: Heat resilience does not require a new siloed fund; it must be mainstreamed into existing
adaptation, DRR, development and social protection envelopes and instruments.
Practically, this means: (i) embedding heat within EW4All implementation and sector programmes (health, energy,
water, agriculture, urban); (ii) using anticipatory finance tied to heat triggers (contingency budgets, climate-responsive
social protection, parametric/sovereign risk tools); (iii) pooling regional investments through mechanisms, such as the
ESCAP Trust Fund to lower unit costs and expand coverage; and (iv) aligning climate-funded projects to heat-ready
MHEWS and health systems.
Investing in heat preparedness can yield triple development dividends
The triple development dividends that emerge from mainstreaming heat into national plans, early warning systems
and fiscal budgets are as follows:
1. Avoided losses through risk management: Scaling heat-health warning systems and linking them to Heat-Health
Action Plans and multi-hazard early warning systems reduces heat-related mortality, emergency visits and
productivity losses by triggering pre-agreed actions (e.g., targeted alerts, cooling centers, work/school schedule
adjustments).
2. Resource efficiency and optimization: Integrating heat information into sector advisories (agriculture, water,
energy) improves operational decisions (planting and irrigation scheduling, reservoir operations, grid load
management), leading to higher yields, fewer service disruptions and lower operating costs when forecasts are
reliable and timely.
3. Development of co-benefits: Heat-aware early warning and planning frameworks strengthen investor confidence
(more predictable delivery), improve social cohesion and trust (accessible, inclusive risk communication), and
reduce the financial burden on vulnerable groups through fewer heat-related illnesses and income shocks.
Heat adaptation does not require exclusive financing, but rather strategic integration into existing adaptation plans,
development funding and policy streams.
vii

chunks.text:
Regional collaboration opportunities on extreme heat in Asia and the Pacific
Meeting the challenge of rising temperatures requires countries to plan locally and nationally, while taking full
advantage of regional cooperation that delivers shared data and services, and common protocols so warnings are early,
leading to timely, financed and thus actionable policies. Regional cooperation through platforms such as Global Heat
Health Information Network (GHHIN) and ESCAP lowers costs and speeds uptake by sharing data, tools and expertise,
while the Regional Climate Centres and Regional Climate Outlook Forums translate global forecasts into national and
local guidance. Early warning alerts should be linked directly to Heat Action Plans so health services can be ramped up,
energy grids can balance demand, schools and workplaces can adjust hours, hospital emergency rooms can deal with
surges and farmers can time irrigation.
In glacier basins, seasonal outlooks from NEACOF, the Third Pole Climate Forum and SASCOF should guide reservoir
releases, irrigation calendars and power-plant operations ahead of hot, low-flow periods. Countries should also share
glacier-lake inventories and monitoring, and run joint drills so glacial lake outburst flood warnings cross borders quickly.
Heat resilience should be funded through existing systems and sector programmes, not a new silo. Consistent with
the Asia-Pacific Disaster Report 2023, countries in the region should benchmark their allocations against the estimated
adaptation costs of 0.49 per cent of regional GDP required for transformative adaptation, recognizing that benefits
exceed costs, once co-benefits are included. Such funding should also include an anticipatory window that is released
automatically when heat thresholds are reached, for example, small contingency budgets, shock-responsive social
protection for households and workers, and parametric insurance that pays out when a heat index is exceeded.
Operational implementation of such provisions will benefit from:
• Pooled regional investments – such as the ESCAP Trust Fund for Tsunami, Disaster and Climate Preparedness – help
countries share costs, use common tools and expand coverage. Climate finance should be directed to heat-ready
early warning and health systems first, where returns are highest. As a benchmark (from the Asia-Pacific Disaster
Report 2023), countries should plan for about 0.49 per cent of regional GDP for transformative adaptation.
• Accurate measurements and progress monitoring – Progress should be tracked with a simple scorecard: who was
reached, how quickly, what losses were avoided, what health gains were achieved and whether benefits were
equitably shared. This requires common understandings and measurements on heatwaves, extreme heat and heat
risk. Accurate reporting and continuous impact-based adaptation go hand in hand.
Leveraging its existing mandates and comparative advantage, ESCAP plans to implement the following initiatives:
1. Scale-up climate-resilient and inclusive social protection schemes: Informed by ongoing pilot initiatives,
ESCAP can provide technical support to countries in designing heat pro-active social protection provisions that
strengthen heat readiness by providing income and non-income support, especially for the poor living in densely
populated urban areas. ESCAP’s support would be primarily through the development of regional guidelines,
policy analysis and tools.
2. Establish cross-border green cooling corridors: Recognizing the benefits of nature-based solutions, through
the North-East Asian Subregional Programme for Environmental Cooperation (NEASPEC), ESCAP can strengthen
collaboration among national governments, municipalities and local communities to create green cooling
cross-border corridors. These corridors cool the air, reduce surface temperatures and provide buffers against
desertification, land degradation, drought and sand and dust storms.
3. Use innovative space solutions for strengthening heat preparedness in early warning systems: It is estimated
that expanding heat-health warning systems in just 57 countries could save approximately 100,000 lives each year.
Despite proven benefits, early warning systems remain incomplete. Only 54 per cent of global meteorological
services issue warnings for extreme temperatures, and even fewer provide alerts for heatwaves or thermal
stress. ESCAP can leverage the Regional Space Applications Programme (RESAP) to promote the sharing of Earth
observation data and technical expertise for mapping and monitoring of heat exposure, vulnerability and urban
heat islands. This information is crucial for the upstream part of the early warning chain – forecasting, quantifying
and timing heat risk with greater precision.
The Asia-Pacific region has had considerable experience with managing cascading disasters. But the rising threat
of extreme heat demands a new level of urgency. Every country needs to act now, with implementation of
regional cooperation initiatives turbocharged to meet the scale of this evolving disaster risk landscape. The above
recommendations are proposed as a direct regional response to the United Nations Secretary-General’s Call to Action
on Extreme Heat. The priority is to move from a reactive to a strategic approach to heat risk management. The ESCAP
secretariat stands ready to support countries in these endeavours as we prepare for an ever hotter world.
viii

chunks.text:
Asia and the Pacific accounts for more than half of the world’s recorded disasters, with a disaster profile,
or ‘riskscape’, that is both complex and continually evolving. Traditionally, the picture was dominated
by tropical cyclones and droughts, but patterns are shifting. Today, rising temperature is exacerbating
climate hazards and extreme events, including extreme heat, which is a fast-growing climate hazard fuelling
heatwaves, floods, droughts, landslides and many other cascading events.
This chapter tracks the trends in climate-related disasters in Asia and the Pacific over the past five decades. In
particular, it looks at the frequency and intensity of extreme heat and projects the future heatscape of the region
together with risk hotspots that are emerging under different warming scenarios.
Disaster trends 1970–2024
Since 1970, the leading causes of fatalities from climate disasters and extreme events in the Asia-Pacific region have
been earthquakes, storms and floods. For the rest of the world, however, the main causes of fatalities have been
droughts, followed by earthquakes and heatwaves (figure 1-1).
Floods affected the largest number of people in the Asia Pacific region, accounting for more than half of the people
impacted, followed by droughts and storms (figure 1-2). In the rest of the world, drought was the primary disaster,
causing the most significant impact on people, followed by floods and storms.
Floods are also the leading cause of economic losses in the Asia-Pacific region, followed by earthquakes and storms.
However, storms were the primary source of economic losses in the rest of the world accounting for nearly 60 per cent
of the total losses (figure 1-3).

Chapter 1: The Asia-Pacific disaster riskscape

chunks.text:
Disaster events in 2024–2025
Over the past five decades, the Asia-Pacific region has been experiencing more frequent and more severe climate-
related extreme events which often overlap and intersect to create multi-hazard events, with widespread, cascading
impacts. Globally, 2024 was the warmest year on record. In Asia and the Pacific, climate change, combined with rapid
urbanization and environmental degradation, has taken a human and economic toll. A series of interlinked events,
including heatwaves, droughts and storms have been compounded by complex, cascading events such as flash
floods caused by glacier melt (figure 1-5).
FIGURE 1‑5 Disasters in Asia and the Pacific, 2024
Source: ESCAP, calculations based on EM-DAT. Accessed on 9 September 2025.
Note: ENEA = East and North-East Asia; NCA = North and Central Asia; SEA = South-East Asia; SSWA = South and South-West Asia.
Disclaimer: The boundaries and names shown and the designations used on this map do not imply official endorsement or acceptance by the United Nations.
Dotted line represents approximately the Line of Control in Jammu and Kashmir agreed upon by India and Pakistan. The final status of Jammu and Kashmir
has not yet been agreed upon by the parties.
In Asia and the Pacific, between 2020 and 2024, the annual average reported disasters per year slightly exceeded the
average of the previous decade, but given the typical delays and gaps in reporting, the real number is expected to
be higher. According to EM-DAT, in 2024 alone, over 180 natural hazards and climate-induced extreme events were
recorded. The majority of events were reported from South-East Asia, followed by South and South-West Asia, though
the number of fatalities and people affected were greatest in South and South-West Asia.

Over the past five decades, the Asia-Pacific region has been experiencing more frequent and more severe climate-

impacts.
Globally, 2024 was the warmest year on record.

FIGURE 1‑5 Disasters in Asia and the Pacific, 2024
Source: ESCAP, calculations based on EM-DAT.

In Asia and the Pacific, between 2020 and 2024, the annual average reported disasters per year slightly exceeded the
average of the previous decade, but given the typical delays and gaps in reporting, the real number is expected to
be higher.

chunks.text:
The deadliest event of 2024 was a cold wave in Afghanistan in March, which resulted in approximately 1,200 fatalities.
This was followed by a heatwave in India that caused around 700 fatalities, and one in Pakistan that led to roughly 550
fatalities. The disaster which affected the largest number of people was a heatwave in Bangladesh in April-May 2024,
impacting approximately 33 million people, with many schools closing. The highest economic losses occured in East
and North-East Asia, mainly due to the January 2024 earthquake in Japan, which caused damage exceeding $15 billion.1
In 2024, sea surface temperatures and global ocean heat content reached record highs, with marine heatwaves
(MHWs) affecting over 10 per cent of global oceans.2 MHW days rose by 240 per cent during 2023–2024, with nearly
10 per cent of oceans recording all-time high sea surface temperatures (SSTs), driven by climate change, El Niño
and low cloud cover.3 MHWs heighten typhoon strength by raising SSTs and reducing mixed layer depth.4 MHWs
intensified 128 tropical cyclones by up to 35 per cent, which reached peak wind speeds of 107 knots, compared to
184 cyclones without MHWs.5
TABLE 1‑1 Key disaster events in Asia and the Pacific, 2024–2025
Geophysical
 
Volcanic eruptions
Indonesia (May 2024 and January 2025) – Mount Marapi: Ash 1.5 km high, 3 km exclusion zone; 15 January 2025.
Mount Ibu: 4 km ash cloud, 3,000 people evacuated, 13,000 people affected; earlier May 2024 eruption displaced
7 villages.1 , 2
Landslides
Papua New Guinea (May 2024) – Struck in northern Province of Egna, burying a village under debris up to 8 m deep
and covering an area of about 90,000 m². Official figures reported around 670 fatalities and more than 2,000 people
were buried alive.3
India (July 2024) – In the state of Kerala, prolonged heavy monsoon rainfall exceeding 500 mm in 48 hours triggered
landslides that caused over 350 deaths and left more than 200 injured.4
Earthquakes
Vanuatu (December 2024) – 7.3 magnitude earthquake struck near Port Vila: 14 fatalities, over 260 injured,
approximately 80,000 people affected (nearly a quarter of the population).5 Direct economic damage was
$197 million with reconstruction costs estimated at around $250 million.6
Hydrometeorological
 
Floods
Iran (May 2024) – Floods in the north-east displaced around 5,200 people; Sistan-Baluchestan: 8 fatalities, extensive
infrastructure damage.7
India (July 2024) – Assam state experienced record monsoon floods in July 2024, affecting over 2.1 million people
resulting in over 50 deaths. Also, in the state of Kerala over 500 mm of rain in 48 hours resulted in over 350 deaths.8
Kazakhstan (Spring 2024) – The worst flooding in 80 years, nearly 20 times the annual average, displacing 120,000
people. These floods, driven by heavy rain and snowmelt, led to dam failures and mass evacuations, especially in
Atyrau and West Kazakhstan.9
Pakistan (Summer 2025) – Monsoon floods caused around 740 fatalities, over 970 injuries, destroyed over 2,400
homes and killed over thousand livestock. Khyber Pakhtunkhwa province reported 368 fatalities, 182 injuries,
and damage to 1,377 homes and 93 schools. Around 2,350 families were displaced and major crop losses were
experienced in the Punjab province. Flooding in Karachi led to six fatalities and caused urban waterlogging.10
Malaysia (Summer 2025) – Severe Flooding in Sabah State displaced over 4,500 people initially and ultimately
affected more than 70,000 individuals across six districts. Floodwaters damaged infrastructure, blocked key roads,
and disrupted access to clean water and essential services.11
Droughts
Afghanistan (2024) – Droughts struck 25 of 34 provinces, affecting over half the population. Many residents in
Kandahar were forced to relocate after wells dried up. Compared with residents currently aged 60, newborns are
expected to experience droughts five times more frequently.12
Pakistan (Winter 2024–2025) – Rainfall was 67 per cent below average, with Sindh at 90 per cent and Punjab at
69 per cent, marking one of the driest periods in history. Wheat production decreased by one-third, sugarcane by
half and citrus fruit exports, including kinnow, declined by one-third.13

chunks.text:
TABLE 1‑1 Key disaster events in Asia and the Pacific, 2024–2025 continued
Meteorological
 
Coldwaves
Mongolia (2024) – Between late 2023 and spring 2024, severe blizzards and extreme cold (dzud) affected over
180,000 people and disrupted education for more than 80,000 children. The disaster caused the deaths of millions
of livestock (over 10 per cent of the national herd) and threatened food security for thousands of herder families.
Impassable roads isolated communities, prompting emergency humanitarian responses including distribution of
fodder, health supplies and psychosocial support.20
Wildfires
The Republic of Korea (2025) – On March 22 the country experienced the largest single forest fire in its history. In
North Gyeongsang province, wildfires doubled in size within a day. The fires were prolonged by ultra-dry conditions
and strong winds, which were linked to climate change, causing severe damage to ecosystems and infrastructure.
At least 27 fatalities were recorded, around 37,000 people were displaced, over 300 structures were destroyed, and
more than 36,000 hectares of land were burned. Over 9,000 personnel and approximately 120 helicopters were
engaged in firefighting.21
Japan (2025) – The rise in heat combined with dry conditions resulted in the spread of wildfires. The wildfire of
2025 in the north-eastern part of the country was the largest since the 1980s . Around 2,100 houses were burned;
over 80 homes damaged; 1,200 people sought refuge in shelters; 2,000 people stayed with relatives; and 2,000
troops and firefighters were mobilized.22
Heatwaves
India (October 2024) – India recorded its highest average temperature since records began in 1901, with several
cities exceeding 50°C. The national mean temperature reached 25.75°C, which was 0.65°C higher than the 1991–
2020 average. October 2024 was the warmest month in 123 years, and Delhi saw a new national record of 52.9°C.23
Between March and June, over 700 heatwave-related fatalities were reported across 17 Indian states.24
Pakistan (May 2024 and 2025) – Extreme heat in May 2024 brought temperatures above 52°C in Sindh province,25
culminating in a deadly wave in June that caused nearly 700 fatalities in just three days.26 In Jacobabad,
temperatures hovered above 40°C and occasionally reached 50°C.27
Thailand (April 2024) – Temperatures exceeded 40°C in 26 provinces, with over 60 heat-related deaths by
May – surpassing the total for 2023.28 Thailand also recorded extreme conditions, with Phuket reaching a
“very dangerous” heat index of 52°C, and Bangkok recording “dangerous” levels.29
The Philippines (March–April 2025) – This was the hottest year on record. Extreme temperatures throughout
March and April 2025 disrupted key economic sectors like fisheries and agriculture, affecting 26 areas and forcing
school closures.30
Kyrgyzstan (2025) – Jalalabad recorded 30.8°C, and parts of Tajikistan and Kyrgyzstan exceeded 30°C at
elevations above 1,000 metres.31
Japan (June-August 2025) – Hottest summer since records began in 1898, with June–August temperatures being
2.36°C above normal, marking the third consecutive summer of record highs.32
Hydrometeorological
 
Cyclones and typhoons
The Philippines – As of September in 2025, the country had been struck by a series of severe storms including
typhoon Danas and tropical storm Wipha, which, combined with the south-west monsoon, triggered deadly
floods and landslides, affected millions of people and resulted in widespread evacuations, school closures and
infrastructure damage.14 Between September and November 2024, the country experienced an unusually active
cyclone season, more than double the seasonal norm, causing approximately $430 million in damages. Cyclones
in the Visayas and Caraga regions, resulted in 6 fatalities and nearly 48,000 were people affected as thousands of
homes and livelihoods were destroyed.15
China – In July 2025, after disrupting Hong Kong, typhoon Wipha struck southern China bringing heavy rain, flooding
and landslides across Guangdong and Guangxi.16 Later that month, typhoon Co-may made landfall in eastern China,
forcing the evacuation of more than 280,000 people in Shanghai, halting flights and ferries, and causing significant
economic disruption.17
Australia – As of April 2025, Northern Australia experienced its most active cyclone season in 19 years, with
12 cyclones, including Zelia and Alfred, which caused insured losses exceeding AUD $1.55 billion and record-high
temperatures.18 As of September 2025, cyclone Errol was the most intense storm of 2025, with winds at 259 km/h
and pressure of 919 millibars (mb) near the coast.19

chunks.subsection: BOX 1‑1 2025 Myanmar earthquake extends to Bangkok, Thailand
chunks.text:
BOX 1‑1 2025 Myanmar earthquake extends to Bangkok, Thailand
On 28 March 2025, a 7.7-magnitude earthquake struck near Mandalay, resulting in approximately 3,800 fatalities,
over 5,100 injuries and at least 116 people reported missing, making it one of the deadliest seismic events in
the country’s recent history. a Economic damages were estimated to be around $11 billion (14 per cent of GDP).
A post-disaster damage assessment in Myanmar, revealed that over 28,000 buildings and 300 transportation
assets, including 10 damaged bridges were impacted. b
The earthquake originated along the Sagaing fault in Myanmar, but impacts reverberated far beyond its borders,
extending over 1,000 km to Bangkok resulting in over 90 fatalities and economic losses estimated to be between
$614 million - $921 million. c , d The effects were amplified in Bangkok due to the city’s soil foundation consisting
of soft marine clay which behaves as a natural transmitter for seismic waves. Similar vulnerabilities exist in other
Asia-Pacific cities including Dhaka, New Delhi, Jakarta, Kuala Lumpur and Singapore. Earthquakes remain one of
the deadliest hazards globally, having caused over 727,000 deaths in Asia and the Pacific since 1970. e
The earthquake revealed the fragility of public infrastructure with average annual earthquake losses estimated
at $179 million in Myanmar and $137 million in Thailand. f In addition, the socioeconomic costs associated with
the loss of critical facilities, such as hospitals, schools, and religious and historic sites are immeasurable. Public
infrastructure should protect vulnerable populations and support recovery, making investment in resilient
design and retrofitting essential. Japan has demonstrated what can be achieved, but resource-constrained
nations must prioritize investing in resilient infrastructure.
Regional cooperation and investments in shared observation networks, complemented by data-sharing across
borders, have also laid the foundation for earthquake early alert systems. Unlike floods or cyclones, the timing of
an earthquake is scientifically much more difficult to predict accurately. Nevertheless, even seconds of warning
can save lives; 70 to 90 per cent of fatalities caused by earthquakes are due to collapsing structures. Initiatives
like the Trust Fund for Tsunami Disaster and Climate Preparedness, the Regional Integrated Multi-Hazard Early
Warning System (RIMES) and Global Earthquake Model’s Open Quake all represent regional commitments to
sharing seismic data across borders to reduce underlying risks.
a S. Omer, “Myanmar earthquake: Facts, FAQs, and how to help”, World Vision, 23 May 2025. Available at https://www.worldvision.org/disaster-relief-
news-stories/myanmar-earthquake-facts-faqs-how-to-help
b United Nations Institute for Training and Research (UNITAR), “Damage Assessment in Sagaing Township, Sagaing District, Sagaing Region,
Myanmar as of 30 March 2025”, 3 April 2025. Available at https://unosat.org/products/4095
c Myanmar Now, “The World Bank estimates the earthquake damage at $11 billion”, 22 May 2025. Available at https://myanmar-now.org/mm/
news/63845/?tztc=1 (translated from Burmese); and H. Pham and others, “Impact of the M7.7 Myanmar Earthquake on Thailand”, US-ASEAN

d The exchange rate of currency conversion is US$ 3.07 per THB 100, as of 11 June 2025.
e Centre for Research on the Epidemiology of Disasters (CRED), “EM-DAT - The International Disaster Database”, 2025.
Available at https://www.emdat.be/. Accessed on 20 July 2025.

chunks.subsection: BOX 1‑2 Climate change and glacier outbursts
chunks.text:
BOX 1‑2 Climate change and glacier outbursts
The Asia-Pacific region is home to the world’s highest mountains and the most extensive glacier systems. Most
of these glaciers are located in the Hindu Kush–Karakoram–Himalaya system, which spans approximately
5 million km² and contains 100,000 km² of glaciers. Encompassing the Tibetan Plateau and the surrounding
Pamir–Hindu Kush Himalaya, Hengduan, Tien Shan and Qilian mountain ranges, this area stores more ice and
snow than any region outside the Arctic and Antarctic, thereby earning the title of the ‘Third Pole’. This is also
one of the world’s most biologically diverse and ecologically fragile zones, known as the ‘water tower of Asia’
because it feeds more than ten major rivers and sustains nearly 2 billion people.
The mountain ranges and glaciers across Asia are highly vulnerable to climatic, environmental and societal
pressures, and are warming at rates that exceed the global average. a It is estimated that by 2050, the world
will be at ‘peak GLOF risk’. b The resulting climate anomalies are interconnected over vast distances through
atmospheric and oceanic circulation systems, known as ‘teleconnections’. For example, extreme heat leads to
cascading effects, such as glacial lake outburst floods (GLOFs) and subsequent events, including landslides, that
can occur many miles away. During a recent GLOF in 2023, in Sikkim, India, a permafrost landslide collapsed
into the South Lhonak lake, generating a tsunami-like wave that travelled 385 km along the Teesta River to
Bangladesh. c In India, the flood caused 48 fatalities, 75 people went missing and 26 individuals suffered serious
injuries. Over 1,600 buildings, 13 per cent of students, 33 schools and 618 farmers were impacted with 497 hectares
of agricultural land fully damaged and a total crop area loss of 502 hectares. Overall, the loss and damage from
this event was estimated at $173 million, and the recovery and reconstruction costs were about $256 million. d
Climate risk information and early warning systems in the Third Pole require integrated multi-temporal
datasets. Downscaling large-scale climate models helps address long-term trends. Seasonal outlooks and other
shorter-term climate information can be obtained from regional and subregional forums, such as the Third
Pole Climate Forum, the North Eurasian Climate Outlook Forum, the South Asia Climate Outlook Forum and the
ASEAN Climate Outlook Forum. Understanding glacier teleconnections and their far-reaching impacts is key to
developing impact-based forecasting systems that enable early and anticipatory action.
a M. Koch and others, “Melting glaciers in Asia: From a cascade of consequences to a cascade of Actions”, United Nations Economic and Social
Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP), blog, 2 May 2025. Available at https://www.unescap.org/blog/melting-glaciers-asia-cascade-
consequences-cascade-actions; and S. Srivastava and others, “An era of extreme heat: Regional solutions for melting glaciers”, United Nations
Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP), blog, 26 May 2025. Available at https://www.unescap.org/blog/era-extreme-
heat-regional-solutions-melting-glaciers
b International Centre for Integrated Mountain Development (ICIMOD), “GLOF from Thyanbo glacial lake sweeps away Thame Village”, 18 August
2024. Available at https://www.icimod.org/press-release/glof-from-thyanbo-glacial-lake-sweeps-away-thame-village/.
c A. Sattar and others, “The Sikkim flood of October 2023: Drivers, causes, and impacts of a multihazard cascade”, Science, vol. 387, No. 6740
(January 2025).
d Government of Sikkim, Post Disaster Needs Assessment: Glacial Lake Outburst Flood, Sikkim – 2023 (2023). Available at https://ssdma.nic.in/
Uploads/PdfFiles/SIKKIM%20PDNA.pdf
Future heat risk hotspots
ESCAP uses the ‘heat index’ to assess future extreme heat risks across Asia and the Pacific. Also known as the ‘apparent
temperature’, the heat index measures how hot it feels when relative humidity is also taken into account.6 A heat
index of 35°C or higher indicates severe heat stress, when prolonged exposure can pose health risks. At 41°C, extended
exposure represents extreme danger with heat stroke becoming likely.7
The period of the year with dangerous heat is termed the ‘heat season’. Data for future projections for heat seasons
across Asia and the Pacific are derived from the World Bank’s Climate Change Knowledge Portal which offers climate
projections for heat indices at a resolution of approximately 25 km. The worst-case projections in this report are based
on the shared socioeconomic pathways (SSP) 5-8.5 climate scenario, which envisions a future with high greenhouse
gas emissions and limited mitigation efforts (box 1-3).8 This suggests that by the end of the century, extreme heat
conditions will increase dramatically in frequency, intensity and geographically across the Asia-Pacific region.
Figure 1-6 maps severe heat stress, showing the number of days in each area when the heat index will be 35°C or
higher. Figure 1-7 distinguishes between areas where this level of heat stress is intensifying and those areas where it
is emerging. Due to their small land areas, Pacific island countries are presented separately in high-resolution map
in figure 1-8.
8
Asia-Pacific Disaster Report 2025

Based on these projections, under the high-emissions SSP5-8.5 scenario, extreme and severe heat conditions will
increase dramatically in frequency, intensity and geographic extent across the Asia-Pacific region by the end of the
century. The number of days exceeding critical thresholds will rise substantially in nearly all subregions, transforming
what were once episodic events into chronic seasonal or even year-round hazards.
A more nuanced picture emerges when using an Index of four heat thresholds – 35°C; 37°C; 39°C; and 41°C, which
the World Bank terms as ‘heat risk index categorization’. This graded risk categorization is particularly relevant for
assessing threats to human and animal health, ranging from sustained heat stress to dangerous, acute exposures
that can lead to heat exhaustion, heatstroke, cardiovascular strain and livestock mortality. Figure 1-12 displays heat
risk index categorization ranging from low to extreme risk. Figure 1-13 distinguishes between areas where this heat
risk index categorization is intensifying and where it is emerging, and figure 1-14 separately presents the Pacific island
countries in a high-resolution map.

FIGURE 1‑14 Heat risk index categorization, emerging and intensifying areas across the Pacific small
island developing States
Within the subregions, the analysis notes the following:
South and South-West Asia – This subregion displays the most alarming intensification of heat risks across all indicators.
Under SSP5-8.5, India, Pakistan and Bangladesh are expected to experience over 300 days annually with a heat index
above 35°C, and in many areas, more than 200 days above 41°C. These regions fall in heat index risk categories 3 and
4, reflecting not just prolonged exposure but also the highest levels of heat. Moreover, parts of Afghanistan will have
100 to 150 days with a heat index above 35°C and could surpass the 41°C threshold frequently enough to push it into
the high-risk categories.
South-East Asia – This subregion has both emerging and intensifying risk. Myanmar, Thailand and Cambodia are
projected to endure over 250 days per year above heat index 35°C and up to 138 days above 41°C. In several densely
populated areas, the heat index risk categorization reaches levels 3 and 4. Parts of Indonesia and the Philippines move
from low or negligible exposure to category 2 or 3, signalling not just new vulnerability but increasingly dangerous
conditions.
East and North-East Asia – Historically less exposed to extreme heat, much of this subregion now faces emerging
risks. China, the Republic of Korea and parts of southern Japan can experience an increase of up to 100 days annually
with a heat index above 35°C and more than 50 days above 41°C. The risk categorization here often shifts from 0 to
2 or 3, indicating that although not at the most extreme, these zones are on a clear trajectory toward higher human
health risks and a strain on urban systems.

chunks.text:
North and Central Asia – This subregion which includes Kazakhstan, Mongolia and the western parts of the Russian
Federation, displays widespread emerging risk. Many areas would see over 100 days per year with heat index above
35°C. These areas shift into risk categories 1 or 2, with early signals of heat index above 41°C becoming increasingly
visible. This is particularly significant for regions not historically adapted to high temperatures, creating challenges for
new policy and planning. Central Asia, including Uzbekistan and Turkmenistan, transitions into both emerging and
intensifying zones, with parts of the region showing 100 to 150 days of heat index above 35°C and surpassing the 41°C
threshold frequently enough to push them into high-risk categories.
Pacific island countries – These are transitioning from low exposure to emerging heat hazards. For example, Nauru,
Kiribati and the Northern Mariana Islands now experience more than 100 days annually with heat index above 35°C.
Most other island States including Fiji, Vanuatu, Tonga and Tuvalu exhibit emerging risk patterns, moving into high-
risk categories for the first time. Increasingly, more locations are projected to have heat indices above 41°C affecting
populations with limited adaptive capacity.
Australia – This is among the most severely affected countries in the region. Northern and eastern Australia are
projected to experience over 300 days with a heat index above 35°C and 150–200 days above 41°C by 2080–2099.
These areas fall consistently into category 4 risk zones and dominate the intensifying hazard maps. This escalation of
both duration and intensity of heat has significant implications for health services, agriculture and energy systems.
Additionally, new research indicates that marine heatwaves can intensify tropical cyclones by up to 35 per cent
(106.72 kts).9 This may lead to cascading hazards and heatwaves, including more severe tropical cyclones affecting
the Pacific SIDS.
Without urgent investment in resilient systems and proactive management, extreme heat could become the region’s
most deadly climate hazard by the end of the century. Chapter 2 examines the likely impact of extreme heat on
people’s health and livelihoods underscoring why decision makers must act now to protect populations, economies
and development gains.

chunks.text:
As indicated in chapter 1, rising temperatures and heatwaves are a hazard with distinct geographic and climatic
characteristics. This chapter examines how the cascading impacts of heat affect human development –
disrupting lives, weakening economies and degrading ecosystems. Extreme heat triggers directly threaten
health, reduce economic productivity, damage critical infrastructure and accelerate environmental degradation
while deepening existing inequalities.
Hotter cities
Urban areas are already at high-risk for extreme heat due to the urban heat island (UHI) effect. Densely built surfaces
trap heat and raise city temperatures above those of surrounding rural areas. This challenge is being exacerbated by
rapid and often unplanned urbanization, coupled with insufficient green spaces (figure 2-1).
FIGURE 2‑1 Urban heat island
Source: ESCAP, adapted from K. Faladlu, “Urban heat island”, World Meteorological Organization (WMO), 2020. Available at https://community.wmo.int/en/
activity-areas/urban/urban-heat-island
Even with a 1.5°C to 2°C increase in global temperatures, many Asian cities are projected to become substantially
hotter. The UHI effect could add an additional 2°C to 7°C, as asphalt, concrete and lack of green cover intensify city
heat. As a result, cities such as Delhi, Bangkok, Manila and Shanghai could routinely experience temperatures that are
several degrees warmer than the global average.10
Rising temperatures are closely linked to deteriorating air quality, creating a dangerous feedback loop that intensifies
both climate change and public health risks. Greater heat increases the frequency of droughts and the intensity of
wildfires, both of which release fine particulate matter (PM 10 and PM 2.5) into the atmosphere. Wildfires further
contribute to pollution by emitting large volumes of carbon dioxide, carbon monoxide, nitrogen oxides and volatile
organic compounds (VOCs), including acroline and formaldehyde. 11 Increased temperatures also accelerate biological
processes that break down organic waste and wastewater, producing additional pollutants and greenhouse gases.
Together, these processes illustrate how global warming directly exacerbates air pollution, compounding risks to
human health, ecosystems and climate resilience.
Figure 2-2 and 2-3 display the extent of heat risk for cities across the Asia-Pacific region. The maps show areas at
greatest risk based on the number of days exceeding heat index thresholds of 35°C and 41°C, using the World Bank
heat risk index categorization detailed in chapter 1. Note that for the Pacific, in the current scenario, heat risk is minimal.

chunks.text:
Extreme heat is thus emerging as a pan-regional urban risk that demands subregion-specific planning and coordinated
regional action.
• East and North-East Asia: Cities such as Seoul, Tokyo and Beijing move from moderate to high risk, with inland cities
facing increasingly prolonged heat extremes.
• North and Central Asia: Previously this subregion had lower exposure but now shows a marked increase in heat risk
across several urban centres, indicating that no subregion is immune.
• The Pacific: There will be a dramatic shift from low to high or very high heat risk in nearly all national capitals by
the end of the century.
• South and South-West Asia: Urban areas such as Delhi, Karachi and Dhaka are already at high risk, which intensifies
under future scenarios, posing significant threats to dense populations and development gains.
• South-East Asia: Widespread and intensifying risk across megacities and secondary cities, especially in low-lying
and rapidly urbanizing areas, such as Manila, Jakarta and Phnom Penh.
Thus, heat stress manifests differently across subregions, underscoring the need for context-specific adaptation
strategies. In South Asia and parts of South-East Asia, rising temperatures combined with high humidity will drive
dangerous heat indices, with populations facing prolonged exposure to extreme hot days above 35°C. By contrast,
many Pacific Island countries may record few, if any, days exceeding this temperature threshold, yet may still experience
escalating risks from higher humidity that significantly amplifies perceived heat stress. South and South-East Asia will
therefore require measures to manage both extreme heat and compounding humidity, while adaptation strategies in
the Pacific must prioritize humidity-driven health risks even in the absence of high absolute temperatures.
Vulnerabilities in energy
When faced with increasingly frequent and severe climate hazards, communities rely on disaster- and climate-resilient
infrastructure for transport, energy and water. However, in urban areas, in particular, these systems and services are
themselves threatened by rising heat. During extreme heat events, the very networks that support city life come
under strain.
Energy infrastructure is often the first to be impacted by heat, as heatwaves drive sharp surges in electricity demand
for air conditioning.12 When power systems fail, the consequences can be life-threatening; populations lose access to
cooling systems and health facilities that do not have dedicated backup power face critical service disruptions.
Air conditioning plays a vital role in protecting human health, especially for vulnerable populations such as children,
the elderly and people with disabilities. As record-breaking heatwaves sweep across the globe, those who can
afford it, particularly the expanding middle class in urban areas, increasingly rely on domestic cooling. However, air
conditioning is highly energy-intensive and, if left unchecked, global energy consumption from air conditioners is
projected to more than triple by 2050.13 Of the world’s total annual CO₂ emissions of around 37 billion metric tons, air
conditioning accounts for roughly 1 billion metric tons.14
Heat itself also affects energy generation. High air temperatures reduce the efficiency of power generation and
transmission. Thermal power plants struggle when cooling water becomes too warm, while heat transmission lines
carry electricity less efficiently and can even sag dangerously under extreme heat. Solar photovoltaic systems are
impacted; for every 1°C rise in air temperature, the efficiency drops by around 0.5 per cent.15
Thus, heatwaves simultaneously boost demand and reduce supply. In cities, this often manifests as brownouts or
infrastructure failures on the hottest days – exactly when reliable power is most needed for cooling and to keep
healthcare facilities operational.16
In Asia and the Pacific, energy facilities are tightly coupled with agricultural supply chains. Heat stress can impair
thermal power output, while heat-driven water scarcity undermines irrigation and food production. To address this
the Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations (FAO) calls for a nexus approach, with integrated planning
across water, energy and food systems to balance competing demands and bolster overall system resilience.17
The Sendai Framework for Disaster Risk Reduction 2015–2030 also provides UN Member States with concrete actions
to protect development gains from the risk of disasters. The framework emphasizes investing in structural, non-
structural and functional measures that address hazard exposure, vulnerability and capacity.18
With this framing in mind, ESCAP has analysed how heat exposure under multiple climate scenarios can impact
power‐generation facilities and assessed the implications for integrated energy–water–food resilience (figure 2-6 and
figure 2-7).
20

Source: ESCAP, adapted from World Bank Heat Index.

Source: ESCAP, adapted from World Bank Heat Index.
Note: ENEA = East and North-East Asia; NCA = North and Central Asia; SEA = South-East Asia; SSWA = South and South-West Asia.
Under future climate scenarios, the proportion of power plants exposed to days exceeding 40°C climbs markedly.
Under the moderate‐emissions pathway (SSP3-7.0), exposure rises from nearly 0 per cent historically to around
3 per cent in the medium term and 5 per cent in the long term. Under the high-emissions pathway (SSP5-8.5), long-
term exposure more than doubles to 8 per cent. A similar pattern is observed for days above 45°C, where exposure
increases from 0.5 per cent historically to 1 per cent under SSP3-7.0 (long term) and 1.3 per cent under SSP5-8.5
(long term). These trends signal a growing risk of reduced power-generation capacity, accelerated wear and tear of
equipment, and challenges with water-cooling systems.
• South and South‐West Asia: This subregion is expected to experience the highest escalation in risks to power-
generation facilities from heatwaves. Under high‐emissions scenarios, exposure to days over 40°C rises from
roughly 7 per cent historically to over 20 per cent by 2099.
» Exposure to days above 45°C climbs from below 1 per cent to nearly 13 per cent.
• South‐East Asia: Exposure, which historically was negligible, grows to around 8 per cent for days above 40°C by
2099, while extreme‐heat days exceeding 45°C remain rare, but are increasing.
• Pacific Islands: Moderate increases to roughly 3-4 per cent for 40°C days and up to 1 per cent for 45°C days.
• North and Central Asia – Historically minimal, however this subregion can experience heat risks of up to 2 per cent
exposure for 40°C days and 0.6 per cent for 45°C under worst‐case pathways.
• East and North‐East Asia – The subregion is the least affected, with long‐term exposure under 1.5 per cent for 40 °C
days and under 0.5 per cent for 45°C days.

People at risk
The following figures show, by subregion, populations experiencing extreme heat stress (heat index threshold above
35°C) and those under severe stress (heat index threshold above 41°C). The data considers two climate scenarios:
SSP3-7.0, where there is little progress in combating climate change; and SSP5-8.5, in which climate change accelerates,
driven by heavy use of fossil fuels. For each scenario, the figures also consider short-term and long-term impacts.
FIGURE 2‑8 Population exposure to extreme heat under two climate scenarios in East and North-East Asia
Source: ESCAP, adapted from World Bank Heat Index.
Figure 2-8 illustrates that under the SSP5-8.5 long-term scenario, population exposure rises steeply, with over
30 per cent projected to be exposed to heat indices above 35°C. This percentage is more than double the proportion
projected under SSP3-7.0 scenario.
BOX 2‑1 Surging electricity demand and the climate–health–energy nexus
Electricity demand in the Asia-Pacific region is projected to surge dramatically by 2050 – driven by rising
urbanization, heat stress and a region-wide shift toward electrification in buildings, transport and industry.
According to Enerdata’s EnerFuture 2023 scenarios, a electricity consumption in the region is expected to nearly
double by 2050, even under climate-compatible pathways. In India and Indonesia, electricity demand is set
to accelerate through 2050, while in China, it will stabilize at historically high levels due to prior rapid growth.
Rapid electrification in the transport sector is expected to significantly increase demand for electricity. By
2050, over 50 per cent of all passenger vehicles in India and China are projected to be electric. In Australia and
Japan, more than half of all space heating will be powered by electric heat pumps. The Republic of Korea and
Indonesia are expected to see a doubling of the share of electricity used in industrial processes, rising to more
than 50 per cent. These transitions signal a profound structural dependence on reliable electricity supply.
Furthermore, the intensifying reliance on electricity is also driven by extreme heat and as climate-induced
health emergencies become more frequent. Prolonged heatwaves in India — where temperatures regularly
exceed 45°C — are already triggering surges in electricity demand for cooling. In 2024, parts of northern India
experienced brownouts as peak power demand overwhelmed the grid during heat alerts. When electricity
falters under such conditions, public health consequences follow; hospitals lose cooling, urban populations
are exposed to indoor overheating, and the risks of heatstroke, cardiovascular strain and mortality increase,
especially among older persons and outdoor workers.
In the smaller island and least developed countries in South-East Asia and the Pacific, electricity systems are less
robust and more vulnerable to supply shocks. Coupled with limited access to healthcare, the lack of resilient
energy infrastructure exacerbates climate-related health risks. For example, during the 2023 Pacific heat event,
some island communities reported hospital cooling failures and disrupted vaccine storage, which underscore
the cascading risks of power insecurity.
Ensuring energy system resilience underpins the region’s capacity to manage rising heat risks. Investments in
grid modernization, diversification of energy sources, energy storage and climate-proofed power infrastructure
are not only critical for economic sustainability, they are essential for protecting lives in a warming world. b

Health hazards
Across Asia and the Pacific region, the increase in the frequency and intensity of extreme heat events are now creating
a silent but growing health emergency.19 Under all future climate scenarios, heat stress is projected to rise sharply
across Asia and the Pacific, with mortality potentially doubling by 2050.20 The urban poor are highly exposed especially
in densely populated cities where extreme heat is amplified by the urban heat island effect amplifies extreme heat.21
Extreme heat disrupts the body’s thermoregulation, straining cardiovascular and respiratory systems, and exacerbating
underlying health conditions.22 , 23 Heat-related illnesses and trauma include kidney injury, heatstroke, and long-term
cognitive and cardiovascular impacts.
Studies across the Asia-Pacific region show elevated risks of hospitalization and mortality during heatwaves, with
particularly strong links to cardiovascular diseases.24 Evidence from Kazakhstan,25 Thailand, Viet Nam and the Pacific
region26 shows clear connections between extreme heat and increased hospital admissions and mortality.
Different socioeconomic groups face unique vulnerabilities to heat stress. Older adults, women in poor housing
conditions, children and those with pre-existing respiratory health conditions face disproportionate risks.27 According
to a 2022 UNICEF report, 559 million children worldwide are currently grappling with frequent heatwaves, a figure
projected to soar to 2.02 billion by 2050. Children’s symptoms are often less recognizable to caregivers, making timely
intervention difficult.28
In the Asia-Pacific region, heat stress increasingly undermines educational equity and learning outcomes, particularly
among children in under-resourced communities. Schools in many parts of South, South-East and East Asia lack
adequate passive or active cooling, rendering classrooms unsafe and ineffective during heatwaves. In the Philippines,
for example, class suspensions affected over 3.6 million students when heat indices soared above 50°C.29 During
extreme heat, the ability of students to concentrate and process information declines. Evidence from cognitive and
physiological studies reveal that elevated brain and core temperatures reduce working memory, decision-making
speed and executive control, with these effects manifesting more acutely under the hot and humid conditions that
prevail across Asia.30 Compounding this, schools may shorten instructional hours or close entirely: in 2024 alone,
climate and heat hazards disrupted schooling for at least 50 million students across East Asia and the Pacific,
with heatwaves being the predominant cause of closures.31 Such interruptions create cumulative learning loss,
disproportionately affecting the most vulnerable. Marginalized students already lack access to effective remote
instruction (due to constraining home environments, energy poverty, or poor Internet connectivity), which deepens
existing socioeconomic gaps in educational attainment.32
A systematic review, based on surveys of urban informal workers in nine Asian countries, highlighted how rising
temperatures and worsening air quality put construction workers, rickshaw drivers, street vendors and delivery riders
at growing risk with little protection or policy response.33
Workers in construction, agriculture, transport and informal sectors report fatigue, dehydration, rashes and even
reproductive and menstrual complications, with cascading impacts on productivity, mental health and well-being.34
These health impacts are compounded by poor air quality, lack of workplace protections and inadequate surveillance
systems to capture heat-related illness and mortality. Women in impoverished households also face uneven risks due
to prolonged indoor heat stress, lack of ventilation and unsafe cooking conditions.35
It should be noted, however, that existing mechanisms for recording heat-related fatalities are not robust. In India, for
example, research by an NGO, HeatAction, reveals that such fatalities may be unrecognized or misclassified; often only
the immediate medical cause is recorded, and the role of heat as a trigger is seldom acknowledged.36
At the same time, health facilities may not be able to provide services and protect people when exposed to multiple
hazards, including extreme heat, tsunamis, floods, tropical cyclones and landslides. In many countries in South
and South-East Asia, hundreds of health facilities are located in multi-hazard coastal zones where hazards tend to

chunks.text:
compound with heat. For example, 385 facilities in India lie in multi-hazard coastal zones; 358 in Indonesia; 149 in
Myanmar; and 143 in Thailand.37 Smaller island nations such as the Maldives and Timor-Leste may have fewer facilities,
but a high proportion of these are exposed. To ensure service continuity during extreme heat, all health systems
should be strengthened to be more resilient to climate and disaster risks.
Meeting the growing health challenges of heat stress requires going beyond incremental adaptation.38 Interventions
must include structural changes, such as cool roof technologies, ecosystem-based adaptation in urban settlements
and early warning systems for public health.39 Equally critical are behavioural interventions to strengthen “heat health
literacy” so individuals and communities can recognize and act on risks.40 Regional initiatives that explicitly target the
nexus between climate change, poverty and gender such as the Community Resilience Partnership Program (CRPP)
of the Asian Development Bank are successful examples of how climate adaptation can provide broader benefits
to public health and gender equity.41 By addressing systemic gaps, countries can move beyond a “zero-casualty”
framework toward building holistic resilience that safeguards health and livelihoods, in a warming Asia-Pacific region.
Economic impacts
Intensifying multi-hazard risks, including earthquakes, tsunamis, landslides, floods and cyclonic storms threaten
much of the region’s critical infrastructure, such as transport, energy, water and telecommunications. The potential
damage has been estimated using the Global Risk Model developed under the Global Initiative on Risk Information.42
This probabilistic assessment estimates the long-term expected costs of disasters in terms of the average annual loss
(AAL) (figure 2-13).
Under all future scenarios, total losses are expected to rise markedly from $418 billion under the current climate
scenario to $432 billion under SSP1-2.6, and surge further to $498 billion under SSP5-8.5 reflecting the compounded
impacts of climate change, growing exposure and inadequate adaptation, particularly for the high-carbon, growth-
intensive pathways.

chunks.text:
In terms of economic losses, different hazards impact each infrastructure sector in distinct ways. Floods and winds
contribute to nearly two-thirds of the average annual losses in the power sector, while winds alone account for
approximately two-thirds of losses in the telecommunications sector and more than half of the losses in the oil and
gas, ports and airports sectors. In contrast, landslides and earthquakes are responsible for over 75 per cent of losses
in the road and rail sector, with earthquakes alone contributing to around two-thirds of losses in the water and
wastewater sector.43 Floods remain the dominant hazard across the Asia-Pacific region, contributing the highest
average annual losses in both the current and future scenarios. However, tropical cyclone-related losses are also
surging, particularly in East and North-East Asia, where they have increased by over 60 per cent.
Added to these hazards, extreme heat emerges as a compounding risk: while less visible in direct loss accounting, it
makes infrastructure more vulnerable to other hazards by increasing energy demand, stressing cooling systems and
amplifying cascading impacts during multi-hazard events.
Heat stress also reduces labour productivity. As global temperatures rise, prolonged outdoor work, combined with
high humidity and limited access to adaptive infrastructure causes a substantial increase in lost working hours.44 This
has major implications for economic growth, income inequality and occupational health.45 Across Asia and the Pacific,
working hours lost to heat stress are projected to more than double from 3.75 million to over 8.1 million full-time job
equivalents between 1995 and 2030. This increase is a consequence of persistent exposure to extreme heat in labour-
intensive sectors such as agriculture, construction and industry – particularly in South and South-East Asia, which
have large rural workforces and low adaptation capacity.

Figure 2-14 presents the findings for Asia and the Pacific from a 2019 ILO study, which estimates the percentage
increase in working hours lost due to heat stress from 1995 to 2030 across four sectors: agriculture, construction,
industry and services.

chunks.text:
The results reveal that North and Central Asia and the Pacific are experiencing the most significant surges in working
hour losses. North and Central Asia shows the largest regional increase, with total working hour losses rising by
1.75 per cent, driven by sharp increases in agriculture, industry and construction – each rising by 2.0 per cent or
more – reflecting the growing intensity of heat stress in typically cooler climates. Similarly, the Pacific subregion
experiences a 0.88 per cent increase in total working hour losses, with the most dramatic rises in agriculture and
construction, both increasing by 3.47 per cent, underscoring the acute vulnerability of outdoor workers in tropical
small island environments. In East and North-East Asia, while the total increase is more modest (0.88 per cent), the
services sector demonstrates an extraordinary 10 per cent rise, pointing to rising indoor occupational heat stress in
urban centres with limited adaptation. South and South-West Asia and South-East Asia exhibit lower overall increases
(0.37 per cent and 0.21 per cent, respectively), though sector-specific increases, particularly in services and industry,
suggest growing risks for informal and semi-formal labour markets. These trends highlight the widening impact of
climate-induced heat stress across all economic sectors, with particularly steep increases in regions and sectors that
were historically considered less exposed.
Food system vulnerabilities
Agriculture accounts for more than 25 per cent of the region’s GDP and employs a majority of the rural labour force –
contributing significantly to food security, rural livelihoods and employment.46 This sector now faces unprecedented
pressures from rising temperatures.
Extreme heat depletes soil moisture, intensifies drought and increases evaporation from soil and transpiration
from plants, leading to quicker drying of fields. This reduces crop yields, livestock productivity and labour capacity,
threatening productivity, entrenching rural poverty traps and reversing development gains.47 As temperatures
climb into record territory, crops and livestock are already beginning to show stress. In 2022, in India, for example,
unprecedented heatwaves caused the wheat crop to wither. To safeguard its food supply, India, which is the world’s
second-largest wheat producer, banned wheat exports. With heat occurring during the crucial late growth stage,
wheat yields in northern India and Pakistan dropped in some areas by 10 to 20 per cent.48
To better understand the risk on agriculture and impacts on rural populations, ESCAP has developed the Agricultural
Heat Stress Score (AHSS). The score combines the warm spell duration index (WSDI) — a measure of consecutive
hot days above the 90 th percentile – with structural variables, including the share of agriculture in GDP, the extent of
agricultural employment and the proportion of land used for agriculture.
Figure 2-15 displays the Agriculture Heat Stress Score across ESCAP countries for three timeframes: historical (1995–
2014); projected futures under low (SSP1-2.6) emissions scenarios; and high (SSP5-8.5) emissions scenarios. Countries
are categorized into three risk bands: moderate, high and extreme high, based on percentile rankings of their AHSS
and WSDI values.
Across all scenarios, the five countries that consistently rank at high risk are Afghanistan, India, Pakistan, Nepal and
Bangladesh. Their scores remain high regardless of the emissions trajectory, indicating structural climate vulnerability
tied to both economic dependence and extreme climatic conditions.
• Afghanistan: Among the most climate-exposed countries due to both persistent warming and overwhelming
economic dependence on agriculture.
• India and Pakistan: Little variation between low- and high-emissions scenarios, reinforcing the need for urgent
adaptation measures irrespective of global mitigation success.
• Nepal: Significant exposure to high heat durations, compounded by its topographic and infrastructural limitations.
• Bangladesh: Less dependent economically on agriculture as a proportion of GDP, yet it remains in the top-tier risk
class due to high WSDI values and a large agricultural labour force.
Under SSP5-8.5, Myanmar, Fiji, the Lao People’s Democratic Republic and Uzbekistan show a worsening trajectory of
vulnerability under unmitigated emissions. These countries currently have moderate exposure, but under SSP5-8.5
increasing heat duration combined with structural agricultural dependence places them at growing risk.
Despite lower absolute WSDI values, some Pacific island countries such as Fiji, Papua New Guinea and the Marshall
Islands demonstrate moderate to high future exposure. They rely heavily on agriculture but have limited adaptive
infrastructure or adaptation financing.
Ensuring food security in the Asia-Pacific region will increasingly hinge on how well the region can adapt its agricultural
systems to the new reality of more frequent and intense heat stress.
