Chapter 2: Extreme heat – disrupting lives, economies and ecosystems

chunks.text:
Beyond the thermometer
This chapter has highlighted the widening impact of climate-induced heat stress across all populations and economic
sectors, with particularly steep increases in subregions and sectors that were historically considered less exposed.
Building resilience to future heat stresses requires that health system planning, infrastructure and agriculture
development, and urban planning be grounded in comprehensive assessments that look ‘beyond the thermometer’.
These must take into account heat indices, but also consider the duration of heatwaves and the cascading and
compounding hazards that they trigger. Future strategies must be more integrated and realistic to anticipate and
mitigate the heat that lies ahead.
BOX 2‑2 Farm workers on the frontlines of heat-health risks
Safeguarding farmworkers from escalating heat risks is not only a public health imperative, but also a prerequisite
for sustaining agricultural productivity and resilience under climate change. The health and productivity of
agricultural workers globally has been examined in an analysis of 90 peer-reviewed studies.
Key research findings:
• Heat-related illness is pervasive and underreported: Farmworkers experience significantly higher rates of heat
stress, dehydration and heat-related illness (HRI), often exacerbated by limited access to shade, water and
rest. Many cases go unrecognized or untreated, especially among migrant and informal workers.
• Compounded vulnerability: Workers are affected not only by environmental heat but also by economic
pressure, insecure contracts and inadequate housing, which limits their ability to take preventive measures
or access healthcare.
• Physiological and productivity impacts: Sustained exposure to heat reduces physical performance, increases
core body temperature and impairs decision-making. Evidence suggests that labour productivity may drop
by up to 27 per cent in high heat conditions, affecting both individual incomes and resilience of food systems.
• Disaggregation of disparities: Female workers, minorities and migrant labourers often face greater
occupational heat risk, due to intersecting inequalities in job roles, health access and sociolegal protections.
Recommendations for policy and practice:
• Establish occupational heat standards: Governments can adopt workplace heat safety regulations aligned
with global best practices, including mandatory access to water, rest and shaded areas.
• Invest in health surveillance and worker protection: Better monitoring of HRI incidence, especially among
seasonal and migrant workers, is essential. Integration of climate risk into labour inspections and public
health systems is recommended.
• Recognize social protection as a relevant climate adaptation tool: Use social protection for climate adaptation,
especially for vulnerable populations and facilitate the interoperability between heat and climate databases
and social protection databases.
• Promote climate-resilient work practices: Shifting work hours, providing protective clothing and supporting
mechanization, where feasible, can reduce exposure and improve worker well-being.
• Include farmworkers in national adaptation strategies: Farmworker health should be recognized as integral to
food security, rural livelihoods and just transition goals in climate adaptation planning. a
a M. El Khayat and others, “Impacts of climate change and heat stress on farmworkers’ health: A scoping review”, Frontiers in Public Health, vol. 10
(February 2022).

chunks.text:
Global warming, driven by rising greenhouse gas emissions, is impacting the Earth’s oceans, land, glaciers
and atmosphere. Oceans absorb most of the excess heat, causing sea level rise and biodiversity loss. On
land, rising temperatures lead to aridity and droughts, and intensify urban heat islands, threatening
ecosystems and human health. Higher temperatures are accelerating glacier melt, reducing availability of
freshwater and increasing flood risks. Meanwhile, the atmosphere is experiencing more intense rainfall and
shifting weather patterns. These systems are also closely interconnected, thereby amplifying climate impacts
and highlighting the urgent need to strengthen climate resilience and adaptation.
Over the years, the atmospheric concentrations of CO₂, methane and nitrous oxide – the principal greenhouse gases
(GHGs) – have risen significantly. Due to these gases, the atmosphere traps more heat that would otherwise escape
from the Earth’s surface into space, which in turn raises global surface temperatures (figure 3-1).49 Between 2011 to
2020, the average global surface temperature was 1.1°C above the 1850–1900 level. The increase was more pronounced
over land, at 1.59°C, compared with 0.88°C over the oceans.50
FIGURE 3‑1 Mechanism of global warming
Source: ESCAP.
In terms of total global warming, oceans absorb 91 per cent, land about 5 per cent, glacier melt accounts for 3 per cent
and the atmosphere absorbs 1 per cent.51
OCEANS
Oceans cover more than 70 per cent of the Earth’s surface and hold about 97 per cent of the Earth’s water.52 They
are the Earth’s largest collectors of solar energy and since they can absorb heat without substantially increasing
temperature because water has a high heat capacity compared to the land, they play a vital role in stabilizing the
Earth’s climate system.
A key impact of global warming on the oceans is sea level rise. In 2023, the global average sea level attained its highest
point since satellite observations began in 1993, driven by continued ocean warming and the accelerated loss of ice
from glaciers and ice sheets.53 Over the recent decade (2014–2023), the rate of sea level rise has more than doubled as
compared to what was observed during the initial decade of satellite monitoring (1993–2002).54
LAND
About 30 per cent of the Earth’s surface is land – primarily consisting of forests, grasslands and wetlands. Land-based
ecosystems have absorbed around 30 per cent of the human-induced carbon emissions. However, the capacity of
land to regulate the climate system has itself been reduced by global warming. Higher temperatures evaporate more
surface water, drying out soils and vegetation, which can lead to increasingly frequent droughts and water shortages,
and in the longer term to desertification. Also, changing weather patterns due to global warming can lead to less
precipitation and increased variability in precipitation, contributing to droughts and further desertification. This has
profound social implications, with water scarcity leading to food insecurity and poor sanitation impacting human
health. Water scarcity also displaces populations and increases the risk of conflicts over water resources.
In urban areas, rising surface temperatures are compounded by the urban heat island effect. Compared with natural
surfaces such as forests and water bodies, engineered surfaces, for example, in the form of asphalt and concrete used
for buildings, roads and other infrastructure, tend to absorb and re-emit more of the sun’s heat. Compounded with
heat generated by humans, urban areas become hotter than the surrounding areas, thereby creating ‘heat islands’.
For cities with more than 1 million residents, the urban heat island effect can lead to temperatures that are 1°C to 3°C
higher than in outlying areas – and can go up to 12°C in some locations. The urban heat island effect is amplified by
global warming which not only raises baseline temperatures, but also increases the frequency of extreme weather
events, including heatwaves.

chunks.text:
GLACIERS
One of the most severe consequences of global warming is the retreat and disappearance of glaciers. The year 2023
was the 36 th consecutive year of glacier loss. According to the World Glacier Monitoring Service (WGMS),55 which tracks
annual average glacier loss, the loss registered in the 1980s was 171 millimetres; in the 1990s, 460 millimetres; in the
2000s, 500 millimetres; and in the 2010s, 889 millimetres.56 Overall, in this century, glaciers have lost approximately
5 per cent of their volume globally and regionally from between 2 to 39 per cent.57
Glaciers frequently melt into dammed lakes, which after a while burst their banks leading to sudden glacial lake
outburst floods (GLOFs). GLOFs comprising thousands of tons of meltwater and sediment pose significant risk to
human lives and can damage or destroy downstream infrastructure and ecosystems by destabilizing natural dams.
The glaciers with the highest GLOF risks are in High Mountain Asia, where a total of 9.3 million people are exposed to
2,211 glacial lakes.58
While glaciers store only 2.1 per cent of the Earth’s water, they hold 75 per cent of freshwater.59 Melting glaciers affect
freshwater supplies around the glacier and also in distant downstream locations. After years or decades of rapid
melting, a glacier system reaches ‘peak water’, after which there is less freshwater for drinking and agriculture, or for
hydropower.
ATMOSPHERE
The Earth’s atmosphere is comprised of 78 per cent nitrogen, 21 per cent oxygen and 1 per cent of other gases. The
atmosphere protects all life by maintaining heat, blocking ultraviolet radiation and preventing extreme temperatures
between day and night.60 However, because the sun heats the atmosphere unevenly, air currents form, creating
diverse, complex and continuously changing weather patterns.
The atmosphere also holds water vapour, which it releases as precipitation, typically in the form of rain or snow. The
amount of water vapour in the atmosphere increases as warming evaporates more water from oceans, lakes and land.
This results in more intense precipitation, including heavier rainfall and more frequent, larger storms. Every 1°C rise in
air temperature is associated with a 7 per cent increase in the air’s capacity to hold water vapour.61
Global warming can also change wind patterns and ocean currents, shift storm routes and alter the distribution of
precipitation. Depending on the location, this can lead to wetter or drier conditions; some places might experience
more precipitation while others encounter less rainfall and an increased risk of drought. As temperatures rise, more
precipitation will be in the form of rain rather than snow, reducing snowpack and snowmelt, thereby decreasing water
availability in the summer.
Global warming can alter seasonal patterns, causing shorter winters and earlier springs, together with other disruptions
to the climate cycle. Warmer temperatures can also change atmospheric circulation leading to more frequent or severe
weather extremes including extreme heat (figure 3-2). These changes, in turn, have serious implications for biodiversity.

chunks.subsection: BOX 3‑1 Impacts on ecosystems and biodiversity
chunks.text:
BOX 3‑1 Impacts on ecosystems and biodiversity
For the oceans, land, glaciers and atmosphere, global warming is expected to have negative impacts on
ecosystems and biodiversity:
• Oceans: Carbon dioxide absorption leads to acidification. This, combined with ocean warming and marine
heatwaves (prolonged periods of unusually high sea temperatures) affects marine organisms, increasing the
risk of irreversible loss of marine and coastal ecosystems and impacting biodiversity.
• Land: Changes in temperature and precipitation affect crop yields, leading to food security issues. Some
regions may experience reduced agricultural productivity; others might see shifts in the type of crops that
can be grown. Altered habitats and extreme events can also lead to the loss of animal species.
• Glaciers: Melting glaciers reduce the availability of cold, nutrient-rich water essential for aquatic species,
including some insects and fish that live in alpine streams. Altered water flow and temperature can affect the
composition of plant and animal ecosystems, forcing species to adapt or relocate.
• Atmosphere: Changing weather patterns, such as earlier springs or warmer winters, can disrupt the cycle
of biological events, including migration, breeding or pollination. This can lead to misalignments between
species’ needs and the availability of resources, threatening their reproduction and survival. Also, more
frequent and intense extreme weather events, such as hurricanes, droughts and heatwaves, can lead to
greater mortality, long-term ecological changes and destruction of habitats.
INTERACTIONS BETWEEN OCEANS, LAND, GLACIERS AND ATMOSPHERE
In addition to the individual effects on oceans, land, glaciers and atmosphere, global warming also involves interactions
and feedback between these key components of the Earth’s system – further amplifying the consequences.
Oceans and atmosphere – Heat absorbed by the oceans can be redistributed within the Earth’s system – for example,
through water evaporation which can reheat the atmosphere or lead to ice melt. Higher ocean temperatures create
stronger storms and typhoons, which draw more energy from warmer waters. Changes in ocean temperatures also
affect ocean currents, such as El Niño/La Niña patterns, which influence weather patterns and climates over large
regions. In addition, water vapour –itself a greenhouse gas – leads to additional warming.
Glaciers and oceans – Melting glaciers contribute to rising sea levels. Between 2000 and 2023, the world’s glaciers
collectively lost 6.5 trillion tons of ice, leading to an 18 mm rise in global sea levels, causing coastal erosion and
increased flooding in coastal areas.62 Also, higher ocean temperatures contribute to the melting of glaciers and ice
sheets, which in turn add more freshwater to oceans and can disrupt ocean circulation patterns.
Atmosphere and glaciers – Melting glaciers and ice reduce the Earth’s ‘albedo’ – a measure of how much sunlight
a surface reflects. Snow and ice have much higher albedos than land and reflect much of the solar radiation. Land,
however, has a lower albedo and therefore absorbs more radiation. Warmer land further warms the atmosphere,
which in turn melts more glaciers and ice. In some regions, melting glaciers and ice sheets can also release gases that
lie beneath the ice, particularly methane.
Land and atmosphere – Rising temperatures thaw permafrost, releasing trapped methane and CO₂. These greenhouse
gases then contribute to the warming of the atmosphere and further accelerate permafrost thawing. Atmospheric
warming is amplified by deforestation, land-use changes and wildfires that release greenhouse gases into the
atmosphere. Also, soil moisture influences the rates of water evaporation which affects cloud formation, precipitation
and humidity. Lower soil moisture will reduce evaporation into the atmosphere, reducing precipitation which further
dries the soil, exacerbating drought or heatwave conditions.
Land and oceans – Forests and wetlands act as carbon sinks by absorbing CO₂ from the atmosphere. Deforestation
consequently reduces the Earth’s capacity to absorb CO₂, increasing global warming, which further warms the oceans.
Additionally, when land absorbs less CO₂ more of it stays in the atmosphere, causing the oceans to absorb greater
amount instead, leading to ocean acidification.
Glaciers and land – As temperatures rise, ice and snow melt and expose darker land beneath them. These darker
surfaces absorb more solar radiation, causing further warming and melting of glaciers. The melting of glaciers
increases runoff and flood risks and alters river systems, affecting ecosystems and infrastructure. The loss of glaciers
also influences freshwater storage upstream, which can impact water supplies for downstream ecosystems.
35

Global warming poses significant threats to the oceans, land, glaciers and atmosphere. These impacts are likely to
be further amplified through the interactions among these systems as greenhouse gas emissions continue to rise,
driving additional warming. The following sections use climate projections to analyse how future warming could
affect the oceans, land, glaciers and atmosphere, particularly in terms of sea level rise, land aridity, glacier ice loss and
extreme precipitation.
Projections for oceans – sea level rise
One of the principal threats posed by global warming is sea level rise. This takes place through various paths, including:
• Melting of land ice in Antarctica and Greenland;
• Retreat of glaciers;
• Changes in terrestrial water storage, due to groundwater extraction, dam construction and other land-use activities;
• Ocean dynamics, including thermal expansion of seawater and shifts in ocean circulation; and
• Vertical land motion, such as subsidence or uplift caused by tectonic activity or groundwater withdrawal.
Figure 3-4 illustrates the projected change in sea level between 2005 and 2020 under SSP1-2.6 – used as the proxy for
historical data – and the projected change between 2005 and 2050 under SSP5-8.5 (a high-emission scenario). Under
the SSP5-8.5 scenario, the Asia-Pacific countries and territories projected to experience the largest absolute sea level
changes between 2005 and 2050 are, in descending order, American Samoa, Samoa, the Philippines, Japan and Tonga.
This order – from the largest to the smallest change – is used throughout the chapter. Countries that are expected to
experience the largest percentage change between SSP1-2.6 (2005–2020) and SSP5-8.5 (2005–2050) are Bangladesh,
Myanmar, India, Thailand and Pakistan.

Projections for land – greater aridity
Another major consequence of global warming is the drying of land, driven by increased ‘evapotranspiration’ –
a combined process of evaporation and transpiration that transfers water from the Earth’s surface to the atmosphere.
Rates of evapotranspiration depend on a number of factors including solar radiation, air temperature, relative
humidity, wind speed, vegetation characteristics and agricultural practices.
Land dryness can be measured using the aridity index, which is calculated by dividing mean annual precipitation by
mean annual potential evapotranspiration – the amount of water that would evapotranspire if enough moisture were
available. Figure 3-7 compares the actual aridity index for the period 1970–2000 with the projected index for the period
2041–2060 under the SSP5-8.5 climate scenario.

Projections for glaciers – glacier ice mass
The impact of global warming on glaciers is assessed using the ‘glacier mass balance’. Figure 3-10 projects future
changes under the SSP5-8.5 scenario for 2041–2060. As current data for the ice mass are not available, the previous
projection for the 2000–2020 period under the SSP1-2.6 scenario is used as a proxy.
Out of 58 countries in the Asia-Pacific region, 17 contain glaciers with most of them lying in North and Central Asia and

compared to 2000–2020 under SSP1-2.6: Uzbekistan, Myanmar, Mongolia, Türkiye and Iran (Islamic Republic of ), all of

Based on the findings presented in this chapter, several policy priorities emerge.
• Generate actionable data: Climate data must be made actionable, that is, translated from complex science into
decision-ready information. Tools like the ESCAP Risk and Resilience Portal can play that role and inform investments
in water, food and energy security.
• Protect critical systems: Critical systems must be protected as a priority, especially deltas, glacier-fed basins and
coastal cities. These areas require urgent investment in salinity control, water storage and resilient infrastructure.
Stress-testing of power systems, agriculture and supply chains against rising heat and warming should become
standard practice.
• Ensure equity: Equity must remain at the core of adaptation strategies, ensuring that vulnerable groups such as
delta farmers, mountain communities and small island developing States are protected and prioritized in policy
design and resource allocation.
• Foster regional cooperation: Regional cooperation can help overcome shared risks and unlock collective resilience.
This includes stronger data-sharing arrangements, enhanced transboundary basin governance and new coastal
alliances. These partnerships can offer a practical platform to pool knowledge, resources and finance, including
support for ocean-based climate action and inter-regional cooperation for glacier preservation.
The evidence is clear: risks are accelerating and time is short. Asia-Pacific nations must take the lead in turning science
into action, investments into resilience and cooperation into security. The next decade represents a critical window
for avoiding irreversible losses and securing a just, liveable and climate-resilient future.

chunks.text:
Extreme heat is no longer a seasonal inconvenience. It is a systemic, cross-cutting threat, silently claiming
lives, stressing economies, overwhelming cities and widening inequalities. Yet, heat remains one of the least
governed climate hazards. The key to managing extreme heat is preparing comprehensive heat action plans
to boost long-term heat resilience.
Despite the extent of the threats that extreme heat poses, much of the current global approach to heat risk
management is short-term and reactive: authorities issue warnings, and measure the toll on communities and
economies. The emphasis lies mostly on emergency measures, including hospital readiness, labour protection and
public advisories. This short-term perspective is due to:
• Policy structure: National and state disaster and health authorities are geared up for issuing rapid-response
directives to local bodies. During heat waves, over 70 per cent of local officials follow directives from higher
authorities.
• Institutional constraints: City officials have weak mandates and suffer from poor coordination, staff shortages and
low technical capacity. Long-term heat resilience is further hampered by political inaction, land-use pressures and
fragmented governance.
• Public demand: Under heat stress, people demand immediate action and relief, including water and work breaks.
• Financial constraints: Long-term heat resilience measures can be costly, leading authorities to favour quick fixes
or short-term solutions.
• Knowledge gaps: Lack of knowledge and education regarding the threats and risks posed by extreme heat
or heatwaves. Heat is often underestimated as a threat because people have become accustomed to high
temperatures.
• Immediate emergencies: Sudden-onset hazards, such as floods and cyclones, often overshadow the dangers of
extreme heat and its impact on human health.
There are five interrelated opportunities to build heat resilience in the Asia-Pacific region.
• Strengthen heat action plans locally and across sectors.
• Define thresholds and triggers; assign roles across health, hydrometeorology, labour, education, energy and local
government sectors; pre-agree on financed actions, such as establishing cooling centers, planning for clinical
surge, adjusting school/work schedules, conducting outreach); and embed monitoring, evaluation and iterative
updates.
• Provide inclusive cooling for all settlements, not just cities. This includes passive or nature-based cooling and
thermal standards for homes, public spaces, schools, clinics and workplaces.
• Leverage data and digital tools. Use basic-to-advanced analytics (only where fit-for-purpose) to improve targeting,
operations and grid/load management. Prioritize low-regret actions, such as calibrated forecasts, heat alerts in
local languages, mapped cooling centers, worker protection protocols and school scheduling, before integrating
advanced analytics. Where digital tools are used, adopt clear data-governance safeguards (purpose limitation,
minimal data collection, privacy protection, public oversight) and document decision rules for accountability.
• Enhance public knowledge and outreach regarding the risks associated with heat through education, capacity-
building and awareness-raising.
The transformation toward heat resilience systems
Reactive approaches are important as short-term measures and for incremental preparedness and relief. However,
countries also need to reform underlying structures through transformational, system-wide strategies that build
future, long-term heat resilience.
Heat resilience refers to a system’s capacity to anticipate, withstand, adapt to and recover from extreme heat in
ways that maintain its essential functions; protecting people’s health and strengthening institutions, livelihoods,
local economies, infrastructure and the environment.63 Without a clearly defined transformational agenda, heat risk
governance will miss an important opportunity to institutionalize proactive, strategic and robust urban action –
shifting from incremental and reformist measures to transformative adaptation.

chunks.text:
Table 4-1 outlines a pathway for heat adaptation that shifts from reactive, relief-oriented responses toward proactive
and transformative governance. It emphasizes a continuum from science to policy to action, moving through
incremental, reformist and ultimately to transformative measures. Key enablers include climate risk information
systems, institutional capacity, financing mechanisms and structural reforms in urban planning, health and
labour systems.
Incremental adaptation involves adjustments that help maintain the status quo causing minimal social inertia, such
as increasing public awareness, altering school and work hours, and implementing stand-alone solutions such as
cool roofs. Moving along the continuum, reformist adaptation addresses structural deficiencies in the system. For
example, by developing networks of cooling shelters and enhancing health systems to manage heat emergencies.
At the far end, transformative adaptation entails systemic changes to the underlying values and institutions. Actions
in this category include legally embedding blue-green infrastructure in city planning, amending building codes and
revising labour laws to ensure heat-safe outdoor work environments. Together, these approaches underscore the
need for a layered, progressively ambitious strategy to build long-term urban heat resilience.

COMPONENT 1 OF HEAT RESILIENT SYSTEMS: HEAT ACTION PLANS
The primary vehicle for tackling extreme heat has been through heat action plans (HAPs). Box 4-1 summarizes key
recommendations for the development and implementation of HAPs. A HAP should encompass both preparedness
actions, communication channels, stakeholder coordination mechanisms, and emergency response activities for long-
term heat preparedness. It should also incorporate the latest scientific knowledge, established localized temperature
and humidity thresholds for cities in high-risk areas, and link this data with heat early warning systems, while
leveraging risk analytics to capture granularity and enable prioritization of the most vulnerable populations. Ideally
HAPs will reflect the heat hotspots and clearly outline both top-down and bottom-up coordination mechanisms at
different stage of heat actions.
A recent review of urban HAPs highlights some progress, but this remains largely incremental.64 Challenges include:
1. Most HAPs emphasize inter-agency coordination but lack recommendations to achieve this, leading to fragmented
and disconnected interventions.
2. In some cases, such as Jodhpur, in Rajasthan state in India, there is a designated ‘nodal officer’ (or ‘chief heat
officer’), but heat-related responsibilities are often add-ons to existing roles and there is little institutional capacity
for heat-specific governance.65
3. Extreme heat typically falls outside the remit of disaster risk reduction or meteorological agencies, instead is often
siloed to the health sector.66
4. Capacities of governance structures and financial frameworks continue to reflect the ‘institutional thinness’ of
climate governance, as well as a legacy of relief-oriented disaster response.67 In India, for example, 95 per cent of
HAPs lack detailed assessments of heat risks and vulnerabilities.68
COMPONENT 2 OF HEAT RESILIENT SYSTEMS: HEAT EARLY WARNING SYSTEMS
Effective early warning systems depend on robust supporting infrastructure, policies, and long-term adaptation
measures, which may take years or even decades to develop. An early warning system for heat should:
1. Include heat indices and health indicators, that are monitored and forecastable, establishing context-specific
trigger thresholds for heat warnings across spatial and temporal scales and sectors.
2. Be co-produced with local governments and other partners particularly in the areas of communication,
dissemination and evaluation.
3. Be grounded in early action plans, such as HAPs, to foster city readiness to take timely, targeted and risk-informed
actions in anticipation of and during extreme heat conditions.
BOX 4‑1 Heat resilience: recommendations for local governments
• Heat risk mapping: Governments should create detailed maps combining vulnerability and heat hotspot
data to identify at-risk populations and areas, and the projected duration of heat wave events to enable
proactive and informed decision-making.
• Heat risk strategies and action plans: Develop and integrate heat action plans into comprehensive planning
efforts, including city master plans, development plans, disaster risk reduction (DRR) plans and climate action
plans, rather than addressing building codes in isolation. Outline specific interventions, required resources
and coordination frameworks.
• Legal frameworks for local implementation: Support local governments with clear legal guidelines and
tailored best practices to align local actions with national policies. Establish a Heat Resilience Task Force
along with a designated nodal officer. Create a community engagement and outreach mechanism.
• Update building codes and land-use policies: Revise codes to support heat-resilient infrastructure and urban
design, promoting nature-based solutions such as greening and shading, and airflow improvements. Set
national standards to guide local actions.
• Early warning systems: Include heat indicators in multi-hazard warning systems with clear action guidelines
to enhance preparedness and minimize health risks during extreme heat events. a
• Coordination: Foster regional and national coordination with provincial or state governments and relevant
authorities.

chunks.text:
A systems approach, both within existing structures of early warning systems and in HAPs, can significantly reduce
heat-related illness, mortality and disruption to livelihoods. Research reveals that many life-saving incremental
measures, such as hydration campaigns, targeted public messaging and the activation of cooling centres are simple,
low-cost and highly effective when implemented in advance.69 Expanding heat-health warning systems in just
57 countries could save approximately 100,000 lives each year.70
Despite proven benefits, major coverage gaps remain. Currently, only 54 per cent of global meteorological services
issue warnings for extreme temperatures and even fewer provide alerts for heatwaves or thermal stress. Additionally,
the warning communication is ineffective. A community perception survey conducted in a city in Nepal revealed
that about 75 per cent of respondents from vulnerable groups did not receive any warnings or heat alert messages.71
A major hindrance to developing effective early warning systems is the lack of globally uniform definitions and
measurement criteria. The terms heatwave, heat stress and heat risk are often used interchangeably or defined
according to local conditions, and climatological and impact criteria. Furthermore, countries employ different metrics
to measure heat. There is an overarching need to develop a standardized framework that can adapted to national
contexts.
To improve consistency and comparability across heatwave warning systems, WMO and WHO are promoting
a more harmonized approach. This involves moving away from basic temperature thresholds toward more nuanced
definitions. Heatwaves are now being defined as “local cumulative excess heat during a sequence of unusually hot
days and nights” – a description that better reflects the compounding health and infrastructural stresses. The World
Bank Heat Risk Index category noted in chapters 1 and 2 could be useful for establishing trigger criteria.
COMPONENT 3 OF HEAT RESILIENT SYSTEMS: INCLUSIVE COOLING FOR URBAN AREAS
For its rapidly growing urban populations, the Asia-Pacific region must identify scalable, inclusive cooling strategies
to ensure equitable access to thermal comfort (box 4-3). In developing countries, it is the informal and lower-income
settlements that are more exposed to extreme heat – putting vulnerable communities, including women, at risk.
Higher-income areas usually lie in cooler, greener neighbourhoods, exacerbating issues of equity and environmental
justice. For instance, a study in Bandung, Indonesia, found a temperature difference of up to 7°C between the hottest
and coolest neighbourhoods.72
BOX 4‑2 Measurement of heat risk by country
Australia – The metric used is the extreme heat factor (EHF) based on dry bulb temperature that incorporates
the influence of adaptive capacity. To complement EHF, ongoing efforts aim to incorporate humidity into the
heat-health warning system. a
India – The India Meteorological Department uses station-based observations across the country and has
a framework for issuing heat alerts. This incorporates maximum and minimum temperatures, night temperature,
relative humidity, wind speed and heat persistence. b, c
Singapore – Heat risk is measure using the wet bulb globe temperature (WBGT) index, which accounts for air
temperature, humidity and solar radiation to assess potential heat stress. For public health purposes, the National
Climate Change Secretariat uses the WBGT in real-time advisories and for national risk assessments. For urban
planning, a heat health risk assessment method combines heat exposure (e.g., urban heat island intensity) with
population vulnerability, particularly for the elderly, to guide mitigation and adaptation strategies. d
Viet Nam – In 2018, a six-day advance forecast utilized a heat index and forecast-based financing, where
humanitarian and government funds were released ahead of a hazard, enabling the opening of cooling centres
and buses in previously identified geographically vulnerable hotspots. The Red Cross Climate Centre has
collaborated with Viet Nam and other countries and regions in recent years to develop anticipatory action
plans for heat. e

BOX 4‑3 Energy efficiency solutions
To combat rising temperatures and alleviate pressure on power grids, countries should maximize energy
efficiency. a This should include:
• Minimum energy performance standards: For example, in the United States of America, the European Union,
India and Thailand minimum energy performance standards and energy efficiency labels for appliances such
as air conditioners have significantly reduced energy consumption.
• High-efficiency air conditioners: The typical air conditioner sold today is less than half as efficient as the best
performing models. Consumers can significantly reduce their electricity bills and emissions by choosing
more efficient air-conditioning units.
• Demand-response programmes: These programs allow appliances and cooling equipment to adjust their
energy consumption based on real-time electricity demand and restrict the temperature ranges. This helps
balance the grid during peak periods and offers consumers energy cost savings. In the Republic of Korea,
a pilot programme with intelligent appliances has improved electricity savings by 24 per cent.

BOX 4‑4 Green buildings in Singapore
The urban landscape in Singapore offers a leading example of how green buildings can be designed to enhance
cooling and sustainability. As part of a comprehensive city-wide initiative, the city integrates planning tools
to promote a greener built environment. This includes features such as sky gardens, green facades, horizontal
landscaping, water elements, lawns, trees, planters and urban farms, which are common throughout the city.
Green infrastructure contributes to urban cooling by promoting evapotranspiration, providing shade and
increasing the albedo of built surfaces so that they reflect more heat. Currently, over 40 per cent of the land
area in Singapore is covered by greenery. Under the City in Nature vision – an integral part of the Singapore
Green Plan 2030 – the city aims to green 80 per cent of its buildings by gross floor area by 2030.
While the cooling effects vary depending on factors, such as vegetation type and density, green roofs and
walls in Singapore have been shown to reduce surface temperatures by up to 17°C, contributing to ambient air
temperature reductions of up to 5°C.
A central policy driving this transformation is the Landscaping for Urban Spaces and High-Rises (LUSH)
programme, introduced in 2009. LUSH mandates that new developments incorporate on-site greenery
equivalent in area to the building’s footprint. To encourage adoption, the Government subsidizes up to
50 per cent of the costs for installing green roofs and facades on existing buildings. In addition, developers are
incentivized to include rooftop greenery in new projects through gross floor area exemptions.
Over time, this initiative has spurred innovation and reduced the costs associated with vertical greening
systems. The programme also provides guidance on selecting plant species that offer nutritional or biodiversity
value, further maximizing the environmental and social benefits of green infrastructure. a

Strategies must be adapted to local conditions. In some settings, dense tree canopies may trap heat and raise humidity
levels. Thus, to help disperse trapped hot air, greenery needs to be paired with ventilation solutions (box 4-5).
Above all, it is essential to ensure equitable access. Due to cultural or safety constraints urban parks may effectively
exclude certain groups, such as women. To maximize cooling and realize their full spectrum of co-benefits, the design
and placement of nature-based solutions must be careful, inclusive and context-sensitive.
BOX 4‑5 Indigenous infrastructure cooling methods in Asia and the Pacific
During days when temperatures reach 30°C there is a surge in electricity consumption notably for air
conditioning for buildings. Sustainable alternatives to conventional cooling are demonstrated by indigenous
methods of passive cooling.
Iran (Islamic Republic of): In the city of Yazd, wind catchers regulate internal temperature using natural airflow
without electricity. Also, coloured glass windows filter heat based on near-infrared absorption, with red glass
retaining less heat, contributing to passive cooling.
Central and South Asia: Netted or jaali screens filter sunlight and cool air through compression, reducing
artificial cooling energy use by up to 70 per cent. An example from India is the Rajkumari Ratnavati Girls’ School
in Jaisalmer, which uses traditional passive cooling methods, such as sandstone walls, lime plaster, net screens
(jaali) and high ceilings to naturally lower indoor temperatures by up to 10°C–15°C without air conditioning. a

methods as eco-friendly and economical defences
against extreme weather. These traditional low-tech
but climate-smart designs offer environmentally

and reducing pressure on power grids. b

chunks.text:
COMPONENT 4 OF HEAT RESILIENT SYSTEMS: INTEGRATING TECHNOLOGIES
Integrating enabling technologies into heat resilience strategies provides a critical foundation for responsive, adaptive
and anticipatory urban systems. These technologies function across the heat risk management chain – from early
warning and risk analytics to adaptive infrastructure and real-time decision support.
Smart and heat-resilient cities
Resilience and smart technology can work together. Using a holistic approach, smart cities can manage services
more efficiently – from traffic and waste to energy and safety. However, smart cities are not inherently resilient: when
faced with climate disruption, they need to be not just intelligent, but also adaptable. Smart Cities 1.0 framework
implemented a number of digital technologies for infrastructure, but often in silos. Smart Cities 2.0 can be more
flexible, based on a closer understanding of how people live, move and interact. In Saudi Arabia, for example, smart
city guidelines balance technological innovation with heritage preservation and environmental sustainability.74
Smart cities thrive when data is shared across multiple sectors. Using common and integrated data environments,
digital operations can unlock deeper insights, speed up responses and make systems more robust. For instance, data
from intelligent transport systems can support not only mobility, but public health, carbon reduction and urban
planning across departments. Digital twins and predictive modelling have helped to improve transit systems and
urban planning.
Smart Cities 3.0 recognizes that cities are not just about devices and dashboards, rather about relationships between
people, infrastructure and institutions. Resilience must be embedded from the outset, ensuring cities can adapt,
recover and thrive.75 In a complex, rapidly changing world, the smartest cities will be those that put people first,
integrate systems and plan with resilience at the core.
Cities in the future could predict heatwaves a week in advance, including not only the temperature, but also where
heat will hit hardest, which neighbourhoods will need the most support and the expected strain on the power grid.
This is already becoming possible with AI-driven predictive analytics that use advanced models to synthesize weather
forecasts, combining historical climate patterns and satellite data to provide accurate early warnings. This gives
hospitals time to prepare, utility companies time to balance loads and local governments time to act.
In cities, machine learning (ML) helps map urban heat islands through satellite imagery of green cover, overall land-use
patterns and slum locations. Superimposed maps can then identify specific administrative zones that are vulnerable
to climate risks. Figure 4-6 illustrates such mapping for the city of Nagpur, India. By identifying these vulnerability
zones, planners can target investments in cooling infrastructure, such as by planting trees, installing reflective roofs,
or creating shaded public spaces where they are needed most.

COMPONENT 4 OF HEAT RESILIENT SYSTEMS: INTEGRATING TECHNOLOGIES
Integrating enabling technologies into heat resilience strategies provides a critical foundation for responsive, adaptive
and anticipatory urban systems.

chunks.text:
AI can also be employed in smart energy systems using real-time data to manage electricity more efficiently. During
heatwaves, AI-enabled demand-response programmes can adjust air conditioners, shift energy loads and prevent
grid failures, while helping households save on energy bills. In the Republic of Korea, such systems have already
shown a 24 per cent boost in electricity savings through smart appliances.76
However, most importantly, AI can help protect the most vulnerable populations and can strengthen institutional
capacities. By analysing health records, census data and environmental conditions, public health agencies can
pinpoint the communities most at risk; from the elderly living alone to low-income families without access to cooling.
With such insight, resources like cooling centres, mobile health units and emergency outreach can be deployed more
equitably and effectively.
These technologies are not silver bullets. They must be utilized thoughtfully, with attention to privacy, equity and
access. When harnessed responsibly, data and AI can transform how extreme heat is managed – not reactively, but
proactively. With data as the compass and AI as the engine, cities can become not only smarter, but safer, cooler and
more resilient for all.
BOX 4‑6 AI addressing urban heat islands in South-East Asia
1. Singapore: AI in urban heat insights
The National University of Singapore has pioneered the use of AI and ML to analyse thermal data across the
city. a AI algorithms process satellite imagery to identify heat patterns and create detailed maps of UHI effects.
This data supports urban planners to identify areas that require intervention and assessing the impact of green
spaces on urban temperatures.
2. Bangkok, Thailand: Smart urban planning
The Bangkok Metropolitan Administration employs AI tools to optimize urban design and heat mitigation
strategies. AI analyses environmental data, including temperature, humidity and land use, to enable city
planners to design thermal comfort zones and enhance green spaces where they are most needed. b
3. Jakarta, Indonesia: AI-driven land-use modelling
Jakarta has integrated AI into its smart city initiatives to simulate urban development scenarios. ML models
predict the impact of various development strategies on UHI effects, helping the city plan for sustainable
growth that includes sufficient green spaces and reflective surfaces. c
4. Kuala Lumpur, Malaysia: Urban cooling analytics
Researchers in Kuala Lumpur are using AI to analyse cooling effects from existing and potential urban green
spaces. AI algorithms assess the effectiveness of green roofs, vertical gardens and other cooling interventions
by evaluating their impact on local microclimates and overall urban temperatures. d
5. Ho Chi Minh City, Viet Nam: AI for urban heat mapping
Recent studies in Ho Chi Minh City have utilized AI for developing heat maps to identify UHI hot spots. Using
ML algorithms, researchers analyse satellite imagery and environmental variables to pinpoint areas with high
heat retention, guiding urban planning efforts focused on cooling. e
6. Surabaya, Indonesia: AI for vulnerability assessment
The government of Surabaya collaborates with universities to apply AI in assessing urban vulnerability to
heat events. AI models analyse demographic and environmental data to identify vulnerable populations. This
information is used to prioritize interventions such as the establishment of green spaces and public awareness
campaigns. f

chunks.subsection: BOX 4‑7 Economic value of climate adaptation
chunks.text:
BOX 4‑7 Economic value of climate adaptation
Investment in heat adaptation should be integrated into broader adaptation measures. The economic value
of adaptation has been calculated by the World Resources Institute.a A 2025 study analysed 320 adaptation
investments across 12 countries (2014–2024) and concluded that over a period of 10 years, every $1 invested
yields over $10.50 in benefits with up to 65 per cent of benefits being derived from economic growth and social
improvements, even in the absence of climate disasters. b
Adaptation investments in the health sector, averaging over 78 per cent, offered some of the highest returns
among those analysed. This is because investing in more resilient health systems can save lives, improve public
health and bolster economic productivity, especially for vulnerable populations.
Given that heat triggers many cascading events, the monetary value of its impacts is usually underestimated.
With the rising influence of heatwaves on the health sector, this value is expected to rise even more significantly,
highlighting the importance of investments in the health sector.
Rates of return on adaptation by sector
Source: C. Brandon and others, “The compelling investment case for climate adaptation”, World Resources Institute Working Paper, 3 June 2025.
Available at https://www.wri.org/insights/climate-adaptation-investment-case
Looking ahead, the pay-off from heat adaptation will be realized when policy and financial environments are
reframed and emerging technologies are harnessed to truly optimize the return on investment in sustained and
mainstreamed heat risk management at all levels. Heat adaptation and mitigation is crucial not only to save
lives, but also to drive sustainable growth. c

returns; data gaps persist in measuring social and environmental outcomes; and a lack of standardized reporting hampers comparability and
investor confidence.

chunks.text:
Mainstreaming heat resilience in agriculture: A model for systemic integration
Agriculture is one of the most climate-exposed sectors, within the Asia-Pacific region, with many acute and chronic
stressors. Floods, droughts, heatwaves and storms routinely disrupt agricultural cycles, reduce yields, degrade soil and
water resources, and erode livelihoods. Globally, between 2008 and 2018, agriculture absorbed more than a quarter
of all damages and losses from climate-related disasters.77 These impacts are expected to deepen under warming
trajectories that surpass 1.5°C.78
The high sensitivity of agriculture to temperature fluctuations offers a strategic entry point for action toward targeted
risk reduction, anticipatory measures and climate-resilient development to address multiple stressors, including heat.
This will require integrating more granular climate risk information into decision-making for early warning systems,
including cross-sectoral planning and public investment. Countries could thus transition from reactive crisis response
to proactive risk governance.
NEW MEASUREMENTS FOR ESTIMATING AGRICULTURAL LOSS AND DAMAGE
The transition should be based on a deeper understanding of how climate extremes affect agriculture. Traditional
disaster loss databases focus more on physical infrastructure or direct human harm and often under report slower-
acting impacts such as yield declines, pasture degradation or water stress. These losses frequently fall outside the
scope of post disaster assessments.79 Moreover, conventional loss accounting does not incorporate future climate
conditions or different forms of vulnerability across space and time.
ESCAP’s INsights on Food SystEM Risks (INFER) is a composite, publicly sourced risk assessment framework that
integrates hazard, exposure, vulnerability and adaptive-capacity signals across six food-security dimensions and
three outcome domains; human health and nutrition, ecosystem health and sustainability, and shared prosperity.
Because it enables country-level comparison and time-series tracking, INFER can serve as an ex-ante measurement for
agricultural loss and damage by: (i) identifying heat/drought-exposed agrarian hotspots where expected losses are
structurally high; (ii) supplying normalized risk scores that can be mapped to sectoral exposure (crops, labour, inputs)
and vulnerability functions to derive expected-loss baselines; and (iii) attributing changes in expected losses to policy
or investment shifts when scores improve over time. In practice, INFER’s country profiles can be linked with Sendai/
FAO damage-and-loss accounting to convert risk scores into loss proxies (e.g., avoided yield loss, reduced outage or
input-price shocks), thereby strengthening loss and damage estimates where observed data are sparse or lagged.80
To project agricultural loss and damage, Governments can use models that simulate plausible losses across different
agro-ecological zones and timeframes based on the 1.5°C or 2°C warming scenarios.81 These models can integrate data
related to climate hazards, such as rainfall anomalies, temperature extremes and drought frequency, and map these
to exposure layers including crop types, land use and irrigation. They can also incorporate infrastructure vulnerability
indicators, such as food insecurity and land degradation.
These projections provide crucial inputs for national adaptation plans and climate budgeting, and can also inform
applications to climate finance mechanisms, such as the Green Climate Fund and the Loss and Damage Fund.
Nevertheless, these approaches can be difficult to operationalize. Many countries in the region lack spatially-resolved
agricultural data, particularly at the district or watershed level. Coordination is often fragmented across ministries
of agriculture, planning, meteorology and disaster management. Furthermore, government officials may have
limited capacities to interpret climate data and integrate this into policymaking. Governments will therefore need to
strengthen their institutional ecosystems for agricultural loss and damage estimation.
CROSS-SECTORAL IMPLICATIONS FOR AGRICULTURE
Agriculture does not of course operate in isolation. It is intricately linked with other climate-sensitive systems,
particularly water and energy. Irrigation, agroprocessing and cold storage depend heavily on access to electricity,
while the availability of hydropower in turn is shaped by seasonal rainfall patterns and upstream water flows. A single
climate shock, such as a drought, can disrupt electricity generation, constrain irrigation and trigger cascading impacts
across food systems.82
Recognizing these interdependencies, countries are beginning to adopt integrated risk assessment frameworks that
cut across sectoral silos. These frameworks employ geospatial overlays to identify “nexus hotspots” where climate
hazards intersect with essential infrastructure for agriculture and energy.83 For example, officials can map areas of
declining rainfall alongside hydropower catchments and irrigated farmland, and pinpoint places where energy and
food production may be simultaneously at risk. Such insights are critical for designing co-beneficial resilience strategies,
such as solar-powered irrigation, decentralized energy systems, or climate-smart land and water management.
Importantly, integrated risk assessments also support inter-ministerial dialogue and coordinated planning.
Policymakers can anticipate unintended consequences of adaptation investments, such as overreliance on hydropower
for food systems in water-stressed regions, and prioritize interventions that build cross-sectoral resilience.
55

chunks.text:
DIGITAL TOOLS FOR CLIMATE-SMART AGRICULTURE
Translating climate risk analysis into policy and investment decisions will require new skills and capacities, together
with the full use of digital decision-support systems that integrate downscaled climate projections with sector-
specific data digital technologies. User-friendly tools can be utilized to present complex data in actionable formats.84
Ministries of agriculture, planning and finance can use these tools to assess how specific crops, regions or communities
are affected by rising temperatures or shifting rainfall patterns. Climate scenarios can also be overlayed with other
factors, including agricultural exposure, irrigated areas and staple crops, and socioeconomic vulnerabilities such as
food insecurity and poverty incidence. Informed by scenario-based planning, policymakers can prioritize adaptation
interventions and set more precise budgets, allocate resources for resilient infrastructure or targeted subsidies for
ecosystem restoration.
When paired with institutional capacity-building and strong governance frameworks, such tools shift the focus of
adaptation from reactive responses to proactive, spatially targeted planning. Tools piloted in the region demonstrate
that integrated climate-agriculture platforms can support national adaptation plans (NAPs), sector development
strategies and subnational resilience investments.
In the Asia-Pacific region, several initiatives are advancing this agenda. The Regional Integrated Multi-Hazard Early
Warning System (RIMES) provides expertise in establishing and maintaining regional observation and monitoring
systems. RIMES helps national meteorological and hydrological services (NMHSs) to develop integrated data
management systems and data-sharing mechanisms. By fostering collaboration among NMHSs and intermediary
institutions in agriculture, water resources and disaster management, RIMES co-creates tailored services and decision-
support tools that transform climate data into actionable information for risk-informed agricultural planning.85
Digital tools and services are also available through the Asian Disaster Preparedness Center (ADPC). With support
from the World Bank, its TechEmerge and Climate Innovation Challenge programmes have enabled innovators to
pilot 23 disruptive technology solutions in agriculture across Bangladesh, Bhutan, India, Maldives, Nepal, Pakistan
and Sri Lanka (2021–2022).86 Making use of IoT, AI, drones and deep-learning, these pilots have enhanced climate
analytics, improved decision support and, in remote areas, provided low-tech Wi-Fi enabled radios to ensure access
to vital climate information.
Successful cases have been compiled by the International Telecommunication Union (ITU) on the use of AI and
IoT applications for early pest detection, and to optimize resource use and reduce environmental impacts.87 This
helps farmers and policymakers to detect drought early and manage groundwater, thereby supporting sustainable
livelihoods, irrigation and food production.
The triple development dividend of transformative adaptation
Across urban and rural areas, investing in heat adaptation offers a triple development dividend: first, avoided losses;
second, economic or development gains; third, social and environmental benefits.
FIRST DIVIDEND: AVOIDING LOSSES THROUGH RISK MANAGEMENT
Scaling up health warning systems for heat and integrating them into HAPs could save countless lives annually,
while implementing occupational safety measures could prevent billions of dollars in medical costs and productivity
losses.88 Within the region, this first dividend of resilience was realized through a heat action plan introduced in 2013
in the Indian city of Ahmedabad, which averts an estimated 1,190 heat-related deaths annually in the city alone.89 In
Adelaide, Australia, the city’s heat health warnings have reduced heat-related hospital admissions and ambulance
callouts, with an estimated benefit-cost ratio of 2.0 to 3.3.90
SECOND DIVIDEND: RESOURCE MANAGEMENT AND OPTIMIZATION
The second dividend of resilience encompasses investments in disaster risk reduction methods, such as the use of
satellite imagery, which can provide reliable, timely forecasts to support more efficient and sustainable operations
across the agriculture, water management and energy sectors.
Crop advisory services have boosted yields by an estimated annual $4 billion in India91 and $7.7 billion in China.92
Some studies reveal that a 1 per cent increase in forecast accuracy results in 0.34 per cent increase in crop yields.93
Similarly, earnings for the fishing community can be optimized when supported by fishing zone advisories that take
into account the warming ocean (box 4-8).94
56
Asia-Pacific Disaster Report 2025

chunks.text:
THIRD DIVIDEND: UNLOCKING DEVELOPMENT CO-BENEFITS AND STIMULATING ECONOMIC ACTIVITY
In disaster-prone regions, the constant threat of extreme weather discourages long-term investments, limits
entrepreneurship and shortens planning horizons. By improving hazard detection and forecasting, EWS boosts
confidence for both local and foreign investments. EWS reduces ‘background risk’ and increases the confidence to
unlock development benefits.
A study in the Republic of Korea found that heat-related adaptation plans and policies could provide a threefold
return in economic benefits; $335,500 per 100,000 population.95 The benefits were more noticeable among vulnerable
groups. For older adults aged 60 and above, the annual reduction of economic burden per 100,000 people was
about $643,562.
Beyond economic gains, the third dividend also delivers social and environmental co-benefits. Community
engagement, essential for effective EWS implementation, strengthens social cohesion, and fosters trust and positive
relationships.
Financing heat adaptation
Heat adaptation does not require exclusive financing, rather strategic integration into existing adaptation,
humanitarian and development planning funding and policy streams. At the policy level, heat should be embedded
within National Adaptation Plans, social protection strategies, disaster risk management frameworks, in addition to
local, sectoral and national development planning and other loss and damage frameworks.
One promising model is the National and State Disaster Mitigation Funds in India,96 which can be accessed for heatwave
mitigation similar to other disasters. This allows local governments, private sector entities, NGOs and individuals to
co-finance prevention and mitigation projects, fostering shared responsibility.97 However, many countries lack such
targeted mechanisms and gaps persist in EWS coverage.
Additionally, as rising temperatures and heatwaves have cross-national causes and implications, they need to be
addressed within a regional context, which is the focus of the next chapter.
BOX 4‑8 Unlocking the economic potential of fishing communities in India
Approximately 7 million people living along the Indian coastline, spanning over 8100 km, depend on fishing
for their livelihoods. Rising sea surface temperatures due to global warming are disrupting traditional fish
migration and distribution, making it increasingly difficult for fishing communities to predict where fish will be
located. By utilizing satellites to monitor sea surface temperature, fisherfolk are better able to strategically plan
their activities.
The ESSO-Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services (INCOIS) utilizes remotely sensed data from
various satellites to provide advisories to fishermen daily, with specific references to 586 fish landing centres
for the identification of potential fishing zones (PFZ) areas where fish are likely to congregate due to favourable
environmental conditions.
PFZ maps, as well as PFZ text, are also provided in the native languages of each sector. These multi-lingual
PFZ advisories are disseminated via various modes including telephone, fax, e-mail, websites, the public
service broadcasting channel (Doordarshan), radio, news media and mobile services/mobile applications. The
information has supported coastal areas and fishing communities for over a decade.
A reliable and timely advisory on the potential zones of fish aggregation benefits the fishing community by
reducing the time and effort spent searching for shoals of fish, cutting redundant fuel costs and increasing
time spent yielding product. This, in turn, improves profitability and the socioeconomic status of coastal Indian
fishing communities. Economic analysis indicated that growth rates in gross value added from marine fisheries
GDP can reach up to 7.8 per cent per annum (an increase of 3.9 per cent, indicating a doubling of the growth
rate), with fishing boats getting Rs. 17,820 additional income per trip by using PFZ advisories. Additionally,
reducing fuel consumption provides a secondary benefit: saving one litre of fuel reduces 2.63 kg of carbon
emissions. Based on this figure and the growing international carbon offset trade, the economic benefits over
a 25-year useful life could total Rs 2.84 trillion, together with the obvious environmental benefits of reduced
carbon emissions. a
a Indian National Centre for Ocean Information Services (INCOIS), “Potential Fishing Zone (PFZ) Advisory”, n.d. Available at https://incois.gov.
in/MarineFisheries/PfzAdvisory; National Council of Applied Economic Research (NCAER), Economic Benefits of Dynamic Weather and Ocean
Information and Advisory Services in India (New Delhi, 2015).

chunks.subsection: BOX 4‑9 Shaping systemic resilience in climate-sensitive sectors in Mongolia, Lao People’s
chunks.text:
BOX 4‑9 Shaping systemic resilience in climate-sensitive sectors in Mongolia, Lao People’s
Democratic Republic and Bhutan
Climate risks to energy and agriculture sectors are rising sharply, threatening livelihoods, food security and
economic stability. a However, national disaster risk management strategies remain largely reactive, with sectors
continuing to operate in silos. b With better cross-sectoral collaboration, anticipatory action becomes possible.
This can be achieved, for example, by managing demand in energy systems or by implementing climate-smart
agriculture practices tailored to local agroclimatic conditions such as drought-tolerant rice varieties, integrated
farming systems or adjusted planting calendars.
ESCAP is supporting governments to transition into this integrated approach. In Mongolia, Lao People’s
Democratic Republic and Bhutan, ESCAP has brought together energy, agriculture and disaster management
authorities through national workshops. c, d, e These convergence platforms have been used to:
• Share best practices for building systemic resilience in climate-sensitive sectors, with government
departments presenting their ongoing work and available data.
• Understand data and methodological gaps in loss and damage (L&D) estimation.
• Discuss challenges in data interoperability and sectoral coordination.
• Co-develop forward-looking strategies for mainstreaming climate and disaster risk into energy and
agriculture planning.
ESCAP has also provided technical analytics, including energy risk hotspot mapping and estimation of future
potential loss and damage in crops and livestock, based on projected hazard scenarios. These are complemented
by digital tools, such as the ESCAP Risk and Resilience Portal, which offer downscaled climate projections and
hazard overlays, and the INFER methodology, which identifies food system hotspots by combining hazard,
sensitivity and production data. At a time when compounded risks are increasing, these efforts show that
resilience can be strengthened by turning risk information into practical actionable insights that can be
embedded into sectoral planning.
By mainstreaming solutions, leveraging technology, financing adaptation and prioritizing vulnerable
communities, countries can turn strategies for combating rising heat into opportunities for sustainable,
equitable development. Investing in heat resilience is not optional – it is urgent and cost-effective.

The United Nations Secretary-General’s Call to Action on Extreme Heat urges a shift from reactive measures
to a strategic, whole-of-system approach that protects vulnerable populations. Chapters 1-4 of this report
discussed why Asia and the Pacific cannot rely on country-by-country efforts alone: extreme heat cascades
across borders and sectors, interacting with floods, droughts, cyclones, landslides, sea level rise, aridity, glacier
mass loss and urban air quality, thereby creating compounding risks to health, energy, water, food and cities.
This chapter consolidates key initiatives into one regional heat-resilience framework that connects global guidance
to subregional mechanisms and national/local execution. To support the UN Secretary-General’s Call to Action on
Extreme Heat, UNDRR, WMO, WHO and the Global Heat Health Information Network are developing a Common
Framework for Extreme Heat Risk Governance.98 ESCAP and its partners can translate that framework into practice by:
• embedding heat into multi-hazard early warning systems (MHEWS);
• aligning sector policies (health, energy, water, agriculture, urban); and
• scaling anticipatory finance and social protection needs that trigger with heat indices.
Heat-ready multi-hazard early warning systems (MHEWS)
In the first two decades of the century, records from four multilateral geospatial data-sharing mechanisms for post-
disaster response – namely the Copernicus Emergency Management Service (CEMS), the International Charter
‘Space and Major Disasters’ (IDC), Sentinel Asia (SA), and the United Nations Satellite Centre (UNOSAT) – show that
approximately 49 per cent of the 2,788 data requests were from the ESCAP members. The three most frequently
requested countries were the Philippines, Indonesia and Viet Nam, which together accounted for 15 per cent of
all requests.

at https://sentinel-asia.org/ (accessed on 9 September 2025); European Commission, “Copernicus Emergency Management Service (EMS)”, database, 2025.
Available at https://emergency.copernicus.eu/ (accessed on 9 October 2025); United Nations Institute for Training and Research (UNITAR), “UNOSAT: From

Effective early warning systems (EWS) for all are a key component in preventing hazards from escalating into disasters.
While traditional EWS have focused on single, episodic hazards and lifesaving response, the multi-hazard riskscape of
the Asia-Pacific region demands solutions that are transboundary and support planning and resource management
in the health, energy, water, agriculture and education sectors. 99

chunks.text:
To meet this challenge, EWS must evolve into multidimensional systems that can address the growing complexity and
interconnectedness of climate risks (figure 5-2).100
• Multi-hazard: Integrate heat indices and health triggers alongside hydrometeorological and geophysical hazards,
especially in downstream layers (message design, outreach, preparedness and early action) where heat-related
health protection happens.
• Multi-scale: Cascade information from global centers to WMO Regional Climate Centres (RCCs) and Regional
Climate Outlook Forums (RCOFs) to national/local levels, linking seasonal outlooks with weekly/daily operations
(e.g., RIMES).
• Multipurpose: Inform power grid balancing, school/work adjustments, hospital surge, labour protections and
irrigation scheduling.
• Transboundary: Share products, expertise and service ‘hubs’; harmonize protocols; enable mutual support for
regional events.
• Pooled resources: By pooling resources, regional EWS can reduce individual country costs by sharing technology,
infrastructure and forecasting data, including access to advanced technological solutions such as AI. Furthermore,
pooling expertise; enhancing disaster preparedness by enabling coordinated, real-time risk monitoring across
borders; attracting greater financial and technical support from international organizations and development
partners; and maintaining robust operations through intergovernmental governance, can also strengthen disaster
preparedness.
• A regional framework: Deliver economies of scale and interoperability through shared observations, RCC/RCOF
products, impact libraries and common alerting protocols; lower per-country costs; and increase coverage.
FIGURE 5‑2 From essential to multidimensional early warning systems
Source: ESCAP, 2025. Presentation at the RIMES 4th Ministerial Conference, 7 May 2025, Colombo, Sri Lanka.
In this regard, WMO Regional Climate Centres (RCCs) can be pivotal in the ‘cascading’ forecast processes which
downscale global data to regional and national levels. RCCs can develop climate products, build capacity for national
meteorological services, and, crucially, organize Regional Climate Outlook Forums which serve as unique platforms
for dialogue between the providers and end-users of climate information.
Thus, a regional framework can be established where RCC’s develop regional climate products and build NMHS
capacity; RCOFs convene providers and users; impact matrices are co-produced and linked to pre-agreed early actions
through HAPs, enabling timely and adequately financed response mechanisms.
The value of such cooperation has been demonstrated by collaboration across the region by RCCs, hydrometeorological
and other agencies in translating global El Niño-Southern Oscillation (ENSO) forecasts into regional and local outlooks.
This collaborative approach not only strengthens resilience but also reduces per-country costs, increases coverage,
accelerates learning cycles and improves the return on investments. The Trust Fund for Tsunami, Disaster and Climate
Preparedness is an example of pooled investments generating higher rates of benefits that extend to countries
beyond those who initially invested (box 5-1).

chunks.text:
Regional collaboration on heat health
Heat-health capacity in the region remains under-funded and fragmented, despite clear needs highlighted in
chapters 2 and 4. A practical pathway can be to define a minimum viable heat-health service package that countries
can localize, while pooling training, analytics and tools through regional hubs with:
• common alert tiers linked to heat indices and health outcomes;101
• clinical surge and continuity protocols;102
• worker and school adaptations (rest-shade-water; flexible hours; class adjustments);103 and
• local-language communication playbooks targeting vulnerable groups.104
Platforms and regional agreements to build these on include:
• Global Heat Health Information Network: GHHIN is a UN-recognized, independent, voluntary and member-driven
forum, co-sponsored by WHO and WMO, that is dedicated to improving global capacity to protect populations
from extreme heat risks. The network fosters collaboration among scientists, practitioners and policymakers
through open access resources, public events and inclusive learning.
BOX 5‑1 ESCAP’s contributions to Early Warnings for All
In November 2022, the UN Secretary-General launched the Early Warnings for All (EW4All) initiative. Early
Warnings for All is built on four pillars:
• Disaster risk knowledge and management
• Detection, observation, monitoring, analysis and forecasting
• Warning dissemination and communication
• Preparedness and response capabilities
EW4All is a key strategic priority of ESCAP’s Trust Fund for Tsunami, Disaster and Climate Preparedness.
Over $16 million has been invested, with 44 per cent dedicated to monitoring and observations; 25 per cent
to communication; 19 per cent to risk knowledge; and 12 per cent to response and common alert protocols.
Prioritizing South-South and triangular cooperation, these investments have enabled innovative pilot initiatives,
scaled up successful early warning systems and strengthened regional cooperation, directly benefiting
23 countries, most of which are LDCs and SIDS.
ESCAP has actively contributed at both regional and country levels.
For example, ESCAP maintains longstanding partnerships with several Regional Climate Outlook Forums
(RCOFs), including:
• ASEAN Climate Outlook Forum (ASEANCOF)
• Forum on Regional Climate Monitoring-Assessment-Prediction for Asia (FOCRAII)
• North Eurasian Climate Outlook Forum (NEACOF)
• South Asian Climate Outlook Forum (SASCOF)
• Third Pole Climate Forum (TPCF)
Leveraging seasonal climate forecasts from these forums, ESCAP provides impact-based forecasting to
assess potential impacts, equipping countries with actionable insights. ESCAP has developed and delivered
downscaled climate and hazard projection data including projections for temperature, precipitation, heatwaves,
floods, droughts, landslides, glacial lake outburst floods, surface winds and sea level rise in countries such as
Cambodia, the Maldives, Nepal and Tajikistan.
For detection, observation, monitoring, analysis and forecasting, ESCAP has conducted hands-on training
workshops on impact-based forecasting. These sessions incorporate both subregional seasonal forecasting
data and locally available data to enhance national capacity for anticipatory planning. ESCAP hosts the Asia-
Pacific EW4All webinar series to address knowledge gaps to accelerate implementation of MHEWSs. a
a United Nations Economic and Social Commission for Asia and the Pacific (ESCAP), “ESCAP’s Contributions to Early Warnings for All”, Risk and
Resilience Portal, 2025c. Available at https://rrp.unescap.org/early-warning-all-ew4all
