chunks.text:
• Global Heat Health Information Network, South-East Asia: The South-East Asia Hub was launched in 2025 at the
Yong Loo Lin School of Medicine, National University of Singapore. The first forum was held with a focus on urban
heat management, heat-related workplace risks, and cultural and traditional cooling practices. The South Asia Hub
of the GHHIN is also being established.
• ADB H.E.A.T. Framework – The Asian Development Bank has developed the H.E.A.T. Framework to mainstream heat-
health efforts into national and regional policies and integrate heat resilience into health action plans across all
government levels.
• ESCAP Resolution 79/1 “Accelerating climate action in Asia and the Pacific for sustainable development” (E/ESCAP/
RES/79/1) and the mandate for a regional strategy to achieve early warnings for all by 2027 in Asia and the Pacific
were adopted by member States at the Eighth session of the Committee on Disaster Risk Reduction.105
Regional collaboration on oceans and glaciers
Marine heatwaves and sea level rise, both intensified by rising atmospheric and oceanic temperatures, are reshaping
coastal risk in Asia and the Pacific. As discussed in chapter 3, warmer seas sap oxygen, bleach reefs and elevate
baseline sea levels, which in turn amplifies storm surge and saline intrusion into aquifers and deltas. These ocean
changes worsen coastal hazards; a hot spell coinciding with a king tide and a tropical cyclone can translate into heat
stress for people and livestock, cause grid instability from salt-spray and inundation, contaminate water and threaten
health facilities.106 A regional approach is therefore essential, not only to manage heat through the ocean, but also
to manage how heat impacts the ocean-coast interface and cascades through the health, energy, water, food and
urban systems.
Regional cooperation should first strengthen coastal MHEWSs, so they integrate storm surge, wave setup, rainfall,
river discharge and terrestrial heat metrics (e.g., heat index triggers for health and labour). This requires interoperable
coastal observations (tide gauges, wave buoys, radar, satellite products), shared surge and compound flooding
models and common alerting protocols. Local authorities can then translate these into targeted actions, including
pre-positioning medicines and cold-chain backups, staging mobile desalination or water distribution, scheduling
load-shedding to protect hospitals and adjusting port/airport operations under combined heat-surge conditions.107
Nature-based buffers, such as mangroves, reefs, seagrass meadows, beach-dune systems, urban wetlands, should be
scaled where feasible to lower ambient temperatures (through shading and evapotranspiration), cut wave energy,
reduce inundation depth and improve water quality. These need to be paired with health and energy contingencies
(microgrids for clinics, shaded evacuation points with misting and water, reflective/cool pavements on evacuation
routes), so cooling and care continue during coastal shocks.
ESCAP Resolution 79/2 provides the enabling science-policy interface: capacity-building and technology transfer
for coastal monitoring and modeling; regionally consistent, impact-based forecast products that localize heat-
compounded coastal risk; and financing/technical assistance pathways that help countries integrate coastal, urban,
health and energy measures into one plan.
For the Pacific small island developing States and low-lying coasts, priorities include: locally-led warning dissemination
in island languages; contingency water and power for health posts; fishery and tourism advisories tuned to marine
heatwave conditions; and social protection that triggers with coastal heat and surge thresholds (cash or in-kind
support for households, fishers, outdoor workers). The practical test of regional cooperation here is whether shared
data and models reduce per-country costs and accelerate uptake of coastal measures that keep people cool, healthy
and connected even as oceans warm.
Rising heat is also accelerating glacier mass loss across High Mountain Asia and Central Asia, altering the timing and
volume of runoff and increasing the likelihood of GLOFs, with far-reaching downstream consequences as discussed in
chapter 3. Managing these impacts demands transboundary cooperation in glacial basins so countries can anticipate
and act on heat-driven cryosphere changes.
Regionally linking NEACOF, the Third Pole Climate Forum (TPCF) and SASCOF (figure 5-3) provides seasonal and sub-
seasonal climate outlooks that, once translated into impacts through ESCAP’s methodologies, can drive anticipatory
water-energy-food (WEF) decisions. In practice, this means producing impact matrices for glacial regions that turn
forecasts into actionable steps and integrate these into local HAPs, so that people are not stranded in unshaded, heat-
exposed locations during evacuations.
Given that glacial risks are inherently transboundary, cooperation also needs practical data-sharing and joint drills:
common glacier-lake inventories and hazard classifications; interoperable telemetry for lake levels and rainfall; and
standard operating procedures that notify downstream operators (hydropower, irrigation, municipal water) when
upstream thresholds are crossed. For example, most recently, Nepal and China have agreed to share cross-border
information about the risks of GLOFs, in a first step that could be expanded to the national and subnational levels to
reduce deaths and damage.108

chunks.text:
Additionally, social and livelihood dimensions are critical for mountain and downstream communities, and shock-
responsive safety protection, tied to forecast heat and water deficits, can help buffer income and nutrition during hot,
low-flow seasons. In short, regional collaboration on cryosphere-aware outlooks and impact-based operations
enables countries to manage heat-intensified glacial changes, reducing disaster losses while maintaining functionality
of water, energy and food systems during the hottest periods of the year.
BOX 5‑2 Strategic investments in resilience – 20 years of the Trust Fund for Tsunami Disaster
and Climate Preparedness
The ESCAP Multi-Donor Trust Fund for Tsunami, Disaster and Climate Preparedness has been building resilience
across disaster-prone regions of Asia and the Pacific by strengthening early warning systems and institutional
capacity, and promoting inclusive regional cooperation. Since its establishment in 2005, the Trust Fund has
supported 23 countries with over 30 projects, helping countries with limited resources prepare for and respond
to a growing range of climate and disaster risks.
A 2024 cost-benefit analysis confirms that for every dollar invested, the Trust Fund generated between $3.70 and
$5.50 in benefits. It became cost-effective within six years, and by 2030, its net present value is projected to
exceed $5 million, making the Trust Fund a clear example of how strategic investments in resilience can deliver
sustained socioeconomic returns.
Source: ESCAP, adapted from Y. Aysan, S. Quiroga and X. Yu, “Evaluative review of the Trust Fund for Tsunami, Disaster and Climate Preparedness:
A cost-benefit analysis 2005–2024”, 2025. Available at http://www.unescap.org/kp/2025/evaluative-review-trust-fund-tsunami-disaster-and-climate-
preparedness-cost-benefit
Due to the cooperative nature of the Trust Fund, investments in transboundary solutions have led to benefits
at the national and local level. Countries such as Bangladesh, Sri Lanka and Myanmar have implemented
MHEWS that have helped reduce disaster-related casualties by 30 per cent and avoided damages amounting to
over $54 million. In Myanmar, under the Trust Fund, 27 farmers used 10-day forecasts and rainfall advisories to
adjust irrigation and harvest timing, saving $1,500 in water costs, protecting crops worth $3,500 and preserving
1,927 acres of green gram. In Bangladesh, the energy sector experienced fewer outages due to improved
weather forecasting and agricultural losses were reduced by 15 per cent.
Countries that implemented Trust Fund-supported systems showed measurable decreases in inequality, with
Gini index reductions averaging 10 per cent. Marginalized groups, including women, rural populations and
people with disabilities, have been included in planning and training efforts. Over 80 per cent of planned
projects have been successfully delivered, with high stakeholder satisfaction.
Marking 20 years since the Indian Ocean Tsunami, renewed contributions of over $1.7 million was provided to
the Trust fund from six donors as a reflection of the urgency for preparing for and evolving riskscape across the
region’s coastlines. a

Adaptation and resilience to glacial hazards: Regional cooperation architecture
BOX 5‑3 Building resilience to slow-onset disasters in Central Asia
The ESCAP-led project, “Enhancing knowledge and capacity to build resilience to slow-onset disasters in Central
Asia” focuses on Kazakhstan, Kyrgyzstan and Tajikistan, through a comprehensive strategy that combines
scientific analysis, capacity-building and regional cooperation.
One of the core objectives is to create a network of practitioners to enhance regional and subregional
collaboration. This network brought together key institutions, including the North Eurasian Climate Centre
(NEACC) as a WMO-designated Regional Climate Centre; the Yu. A. Izrael Institute of Global Climate and
Ecology (IGCE); the Centre for Emergency Situations and Disaster Risk Reduction (CESDRR); and national
hydrometeorology services and Emergency Committees (Emercoms) from these target countries and the
Russian Federation. This network fosters cross-border knowledge exchange and facilitates the co-development
of methodologies for climate projections, impact-based forecasting and strategic adaptation planning.
To support this collaboration, the project convened a series of regional, subregional and national policy
dialogues, workshops and trainings, including special sessions during the NEACOF forums and the 2025 Arctic
Summit in Saint Petersburg. Through platforms like NEACOF, participating experts explored how seasonal
outlooks could translate into impact-based forecasting for strategic intervention using ESCAP’s automated
IBF tool.
In parallel, ESCAP, in collaboration with this network, such as NEACC and IGCE has produced a series of joint
publications based on the developed methodologies and the findings to raise awareness and advance scientific
dialogue. These include:
• Blog: “The Era of Extreme Heat: Teleconnections, Glacial Risks, and the Arctic-Third Pole” a
• Scientific article: “Arctic to the Himalayas: Teleconnections of Glacial Hazards” (2025), which explores how
warming in the Arctic affects glacier stability and water security across the Third Pole. b
By synergizing the available data and leveraging the expertise of this network to create data-driven and risk-
informed strategies and recommendations, the project laid the groundwork for regional resilience to slow-
onset and emerging risks, targeting a long-term approach to disaster risk resilience in Central Asia. c

A strategic framework to strengthen regional solutions
Asia and the Pacific needs an integrated regional heat-resilience framework that turns the science discussed in
chapters 1-4 into coordinated, financed action. As a starting point, the proposed framework begins with protecting
health and is organized around mutually reinforcing thrusts, underpinned by integrated financing:
Practically, this means: (i) embedding heat within EW4All implementation and sector programmes (health, energy,
water, agriculture, urban); (ii) using anticipatory finance tied to heat triggers (contingency budgets, shock-responsive
safety protection, parametric/sovereign risk tools); (iii) pooling regional investments through mechanisms, such as
the ESCAP Trust Fund to lower unit costs and expand coverage; and (iv) aligning climate-funded projects to heat-
ready MHEWS and health systems.
Consistent with the Asia-Pacific Disaster Report 2023, countries in the region should benchmark their allocations
against the estimated adaptation costs of 0.49 per cent of regional GDP required for transformative adaptation,
recognizing that benefits exceed costs, once co-benefits are included. Robust measurement, reporting and learning
that covers reach, timeliness, avoided losses, health outcomes and equity should ensure that programmes adapt
continuously.
Entering an era of rising heat and rising risks, the region’s success hinges on a coherent framework that is mainstreamed,
health-centred, equity-driven and fiscally integrated to translate global commitments into concrete protections for
people and systems across Asia and the Pacific.
1. Heat-ready MHEWSs as shared services:
Agreed alert levels tied to common metrics on heat, interoperable alerting and local-
language communication.
2. Health-centric standard steps:
Standard clinical steps, including continuity during patient surges, for hospitals and
clinics to follow during heat alerts.
3. Integrated planning for cascading risks, with oceans and glaciers treated
as systems impacted by heat:
Cryosphere-aware seasonal outlooks that drive anticipatory water-energy-food
operations related to reservoirs, irrigation calendars, planting advisories and similar
measures.
4. Equity-first social protection:
Climate-responsive social protection with income and non-income support triggered
by heat indices. This includes remunerated provisions for rest-shade-water, flexible
working hours and targeted protocols for women, older persons, children, persons with
disabilities, migrants and outdoor workers.
5. Integrated financing:
Heat does not require a new siloed fund; it must be mainstreamed into existing
adaptation, DRR, development and social protection envelopes and instruments.

chunks.text:
Leveraging its existing mandates and comparative advantage, ESCAP plans to implement the following initiatives:
1. Scale-up Climate Resilient and Inclusive Social Protection schemes: Informed by ongoing
pilot initiatives, ESCAP can provide technical support to countries in devising heat pro-active,
social protection provisions that strengthen heat readiness, by providing income and non-
income support, especially for the poor living in densely populated urban areas. ESCAP’s
support would be primarily through the development of regional guidelines, policy analysis
and tools.
2. Establish cross-border green cooling corridors: Recognizing the benefits of nature-
based solutions, through the North-East Asian Subregional Programme for Environmental
Cooperation (NEASPEC), ESCAP can strengthen collaboration among national governments,
municipalities and local communities to create green cooling cross-border corridors. These
corridors cool the air, reduce surface temperatures and provide buffers against desertification,
land degradation, drought and sand and dust storms.
3. Use innovative space solutions for strengthening heat preparedness in early warning
systems: Despite proven benefits, early warning systems remain incomplete. Only 54 per cent
of global meteorological services issue warnings for extreme temperatures and even fewer
provide alerts for heatwaves or thermal stress. ESCAP can leverage the Regional Space
Applications Programme (RESAP) to promote the sharing of Earth observation data and
technical expertise for mapping and monitoring of heat exposure, vulnerability and urban
heat islands. This information is crucial for the upstream part of the early warning chain —
forecasting, quantifying and timing heat risk with greater precision.
1
2
3
The Asia-Pacific region has had considerable experience with managing cascading disasters. But the rising threat of
extreme heat demands a new level of urgency. Every country needs to act now, with implementation of regional
cooperation initiatives turbocharged to meet the scale of this evolving disaster risk landscape. The above
recommendations are proposed as a direct regional response to the United Nations Secretary-General’s Call to Action
on Extreme Heat. The priority is to move from a reactive to a strategic approach to heat risk management. The ESCAP
secretariat stands ready to support countries in these endeavours as we prepare for an ever hotter world.
